questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-review-1, image 2026-07-07_063330-1.jpg (liferaft parts diagram, items 3 'Recognition light' and 7 'Water activated battery'); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-review-1-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What part of an inflatable liferaft is used to provide protection from cold water?

choices.text: Inflating the water pockets
choices.misconception: Water pockets/ballast bags are for stability, not cold protection.

choices.text: Inflating the inner floor
The raft's floor is covered with thermal reflective material
choices.misconception: Describes a different (and not the marked) insulation approach -- an inflated air layer, not a reflective coating, is the marked answer on this source.

choices.text: The double-layered canopy
choices.misconception: The canopy protects from wind/spray/overhead heat loss, a different exposure direction from the floor.

hints: Think about where cold reaches survivors from -- not just wind and spray, but straight up through what they're sitting on.
questions.explanation: Inflating the liferaft's inner floor is what protects against cold transmitted from the water directly beneath the raft -- separate from the canopy's protection against wind and spray from above.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-1a, image 2026-07-08_044219-2.jpg (Final Review Quiz #1, question 5 of 39); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-1a-source-intake.json Source note: this question's correct answer is flagged medium confidence in the source intake -- the marking shows a clean circle on 'inflating the inner floor' (B) plus a second circle-with-strikethrough on 'the double-layered canopy' (D), read as B being final and D being an eliminated choice, but not 100% certain. Also closely related to the pending draft q-quizr1-floor-vs-canopy (2026-07-08-quiz-review-1 batch), which tests the same floor/canopy distinction from a compare-and-contrast angle rather than this question's single-answer elimination format.

questions.prompt: Which of these items CANNOT be used as a substitute for a missing sea anchor?

choices.text: A bucket
choices.misconception: A bucket, dragged in the water, can create the drag needed to substitute for a sea anchor.

choices.text: Some canvas tied between two oars
choices.misconception: A canvas-and-oar rig can create enough drag to substitute for a sea anchor.

choices.text: An air buoyancy tank filled with air
A few life jackets lashed together and weighted with small shackles
choices.misconception: Weighted, lashed life jackets can create enough drag to substitute for a sea anchor.

hints: A sea anchor works by creating drag in the water -- think about which of these items would float on the surface instead of dragging.
questions.explanation: An air buoyancy tank filled with air would float rather than create drag, so it cannot substitute for a missing sea anchor -- unlike a bucket, a canvas-and-oar rig, or weighted life jackets, all of which can create the drag a sea anchor provides.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-1a, image 2026-07-08_044219-2.jpg (Final Review Quiz #1, question 6 of 39); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-1a-source-intake.json Source note: this question's correct answer is flagged medium confidence in the source intake -- the marking shows a clean circle on 'an air buoyancy tank filled with air' (C) plus a second circle-with-strikethrough on 'a few life jackets lashed together...' (D), read as C being final and D being an eliminated choice, but not 100% certain.

questions.prompt: While running through smooth seas at maximum capacity, what is the maximum speed an enclosed lifeboat can make?

choices.text: 5 knots
choices.misconception: Understates the actual maximum speed figure.

choices.text: 6 knots
7 knots
choices.misconception: Overstates the actual maximum speed figure.

choices.text: 8 knots
choices.misconception: Overstates the actual maximum speed figure.

hints: This figure (6 knots) also happens to be the separate Class 1 motor lifeboat's MINIMUM speed requirement, already live in this course as its own question -- read carefully whether a question is asking about a MAXIMUM or a MINIMUM speed.
questions.explanation: An enclosed lifeboat's maximum speed in smooth seas at maximum capacity is 6 knots. Careful: this is a different claim from the Class 1 motor lifeboat's minimum required speed (also 6 knots) -- same number, different meaning (a ceiling here vs. a floor there).
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-1a, image 2026-07-08_044219-2.jpg (Final Review Quiz #1, question 9 of 39); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-1a-source-intake.json Confusion-risk note: shares the number '6 knots' with the separately-established Class 1 motor lifeboat MINIMUM speed requirement (live q-quizr1-class1-speed, promoted 2026-07-08 from the 2026-07-08-quiz-review-1 batch) -- this question is about a MAXIMUM, that one is about a MINIMUM. Wording checked 2026-07-09 per vinci-held-question-remediation-report.md -- hint updated to name the live cross-reference directly now that it's confirmed live, rather than referring to it vaguely.

Not the number assigned to the heaving line on this diagram.
Not the number assigned to the heaving line on this diagram.

choices.text: Internal lifeline
choices.misconception: Not the item at position 4 on this diagram.

choices.text: Deflation plug
choices.misconception: Not the item at position 4 on this diagram.

choices.text: Pressure relief valve
Inflation floor
choices.misconception: Not the item at position 4 on this diagram.

questions.prompt: A lifeboat is required to have one what located on the bow and stern?

choices.text: First aid kit
choices.misconception: First aid kits are standard lifeboat equipment but not specifically required one-at-bow-one-at-stern.

choices.text: Fire extinguisher
choices.misconception: Fire extinguishers are stowed at each end of engine-powered lifeboats per other course content, but that's a different fact from this question's marked answer.

choices.text: Water catchment system
choices.misconception: Not the marked answer for this specific bow-and-stern requirement.

choices.text: Hatchet

hints: This tool would be useful for quickly cutting lines at either end of the boat during an emergency.
questions.explanation: A lifeboat is required to have one hatchet located at the bow and one at the stern, for quickly cutting lines in an emergency.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-1a, image 2026-07-08_044219-7.jpg (Final Review Quiz #1, question 39 of 39); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-1a-source-intake.json VERIFICATION FLAG (original): this was a specific, somewhat surprising claim not corroborated anywhere else in this repo's content (a fire extinguisher at each end of engine-powered lifeboats IS already established, which this question's choice B deliberately trades on as a plausible-sounding distractor). RESOLVED 2026-07-09 per vinci-held-question-remediation-report.md: independently confirmed by 46 CFR 169.529(k) (Document(7).PDF.pdf, src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-sea-trials-source-intake.json), which states a lifeboat hatchet must be 'readily available for use, one at each end of the lifeboat' -- a second, independent federal-regulation source matching this question's marked answer word-for-word in substance.

questions.prompt: How often must a gun or other explosive signal be fired to count as a recognized distress signal?

choices.text: At intervals of about a minute
Continuously, with no interval between shots
choices.misconception: Describes the separate fog-signaling-apparatus method, not the gun/explosive-signal method.

choices.text: Once only, and never repeated
choices.misconception: A single shot wouldn't reliably be recognized as ongoing distress rather than an isolated, ambiguous noise.

choices.text: At intervals of about an hour
choices.misconception: Far too infrequent to function as a distress signal.

hints: This is meant to be recognizable as a repeating pattern, not a single ambiguous bang.
questions.explanation: Per 33 CFR 87.1(a), a gun or other explosive signal fired at intervals of about a minute is a recognized distress signal.
questions.sourceReference: Sea-trials batch, Document(1).PDF.pdf (33 CFR Part 87, Annex IV: Distress Signals, § 87.1(a)); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-sea-trials-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What spoken word is the recognized radiotelephone distress signal?

choices.text: "Mayday"
"SOS"
choices.misconception: SOS is the Morse Code / radiotelegraphy distress signal, a different signaling method from spoken radiotelephony.

choices.text: "Pan-pan"
choices.misconception: Pan-pan is the urgency signal, one level below distress -- not the distress word itself.

choices.text: "Roger"
choices.misconception: A standard acknowledgment word, not a distress signal.

hints: This word is repeated three times in an actual distress call, though the regulation itself just names the single word.
questions.explanation: Per 33 CFR 87.1(e), a distress signal sent by radiotelephony consists of the spoken word "Mayday."
questions.sourceReference: Sea-trials batch, Document(1).PDF.pdf (33 CFR Part 87, Annex IV: Distress Signals, § 87.1(e)); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-sea-trials-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What does the Morse Code group ". . . -- -- -- . . ." represent as a distress signal?

choices.text: SOS
Mayday
choices.misconception: Mayday is the spoken radiotelephony word, a different signaling method from Morse Code.

choices.text: N.C.
choices.misconception: N.C. is the International Code Signal flag hoist for distress, a different signaling method.

choices.text: CQD
choices.misconception: An older distress call since replaced, not the current recognized signal.

hints: Three short, three long, three short.
questions.explanation: Per 33 CFR 87.1(d), a signal made by radiotelegraphy or any other signaling method consisting of the group ". . . -- -- -- . . ." (SOS) in Morse Code is a recognized distress signal.
questions.sourceReference: Sea-trials batch, Document(1).PDF.pdf (33 CFR Part 87, Annex IV: Distress Signals, § 87.1(d)); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-sea-trials-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What two-letter International Code Signal, displayed as a flag hoist, indicates distress?

choices.text: N.C.
S.O.
choices.misconception: Not a real International Code Signal distress hoist.

choices.text: M.D.
choices.misconception: Invents a code not part of the actual signal system.

choices.text: P.D.
choices.misconception: Invents a code not part of the actual signal system.

hints: Two letters, hoisted as flags -- not related to "no can do" or similar mnemonics.
questions.explanation: Per 33 CFR 87.1(f), the International Code Signal of distress is indicated by the flag hoist N.C.
questions.sourceReference: Sea-trials batch, Document(1).PDF.pdf (33 CFR Part 87, Annex IV: Distress Signals, § 87.1(f)); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-sea-trials-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: Besides flags, flares, and radio signals, what simple shape combination hoisted on a vessel can indicate distress?

choices.text: A square flag having above or below it a ball or anything resembling a ball
A triangular flag with a circle beside it
choices.misconception: Not the shape combination described by the regulation.

choices.text: Two square flags side by side
choices.misconception: Not the shape combination described by the regulation.

choices.text: A ball alone, with no flag
choices.misconception: The regulation requires the flag-plus-ball combination, not the ball by itself.

hints: Think of a flag with a round shape positioned either above or below it.
questions.explanation: Per 33 CFR 87.1(g), a signal consisting of a square flag having above or below it a ball or anything resembling a ball is a recognized distress signal.
questions.sourceReference: Sea-trials batch, Document(1).PDF.pdf (33 CFR Part 87, Annex IV: Distress Signals, § 87.1(g)); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-sea-trials-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What color smoke signal is a recognized distress signal?

choices.text: Orange
Black
choices.misconception: Not the color specified by the regulation for a distress smoke signal.

choices.text: White
choices.misconception: Not the color specified by the regulation for a distress smoke signal.

choices.text: Green
choices.misconception: Not the color specified by the regulation for a distress smoke signal.

hints: Same color family as a liferaft's buoyant smoke signal.
questions.explanation: Per 33 CFR 87.1(j), a smoke signal giving off orange-colored smoke is a recognized distress signal.
questions.sourceReference: Sea-trials batch, Document(1).PDF.pdf (33 CFR Part 87, Annex IV: Distress Signals, § 87.1(j)); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-sea-trials-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: If you have no flares, radio, or any other equipment, what body motion alone can signal distress to a nearby vessel or aircraft?

choices.text: Slowly and repeatedly raising and lowering your arms outstretched to each side
Waving one arm rapidly overhead
choices.misconception: Easily mistaken for a routine greeting or wave, not a designated distress motion.

choices.text: Pointing repeatedly toward the horizon
choices.misconception: Not the recognized distress motion -- doesn't clearly signal an emergency to a distant observer.

choices.text: Clapping your hands above your head
choices.misconception: Not visible or distinctive enough at a distance to serve as a distress signal.

hints: Think of a slow, deliberate, wide motion -- something that reads clearly as different from a casual wave, even from far away.
questions.explanation: Per 33 CFR 87.1(k), slowly and repeatedly raising and lowering arms outstretched to each side is a recognized distress signal -- valuable precisely because it requires no equipment at all.
questions.sourceReference: Sea-trials batch, Document(1).PDF.pdf (33 CFR Part 87, Annex IV: Distress Signals, § 87.1(k)); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-sea-trials-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: A high-intensity white light flashing at what rate is a recognized distress signal?

choices.text: 50 to 70 times per minute
10 to 20 times per minute
choices.misconception: Too slow to match the regulated flash-rate range.

choices.text: 100 to 120 times per minute
choices.misconception: Too fast to match the regulated flash-rate range.

choices.text: Any flash rate qualifies, as long as the light is white
choices.misconception: The regulation specifies a particular rate range, not an unrestricted flash rate.

hints: Roughly once per second, give or take.
questions.explanation: Per 33 CFR 87.1(p), a high intensity white light flashing at regular intervals from 50 to 70 times per minute is a recognized distress signal.
questions.sourceReference: Sea-trials batch, Document(1).PDF.pdf (33 CFR Part 87, Annex IV: Distress Signals, § 87.1(p)); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-sea-trials-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What is the required primary exterior color of an approved immersion suit?

choices.text: Vivid reddish orange
Safety yellow
choices.misconception: A common high-visibility color, but not the specific color the regulation requires for immersion suits.

choices.text: The same International Orange used for life preservers, since both are lifesaving gear
choices.misconception: Immersion suits have their own specifically-named color requirement, not simply the life preserver color reused.

choices.text: Black, for heat absorption
choices.misconception: Black would work against the suit's high-visibility rescue purpose.

hints: Compare this to the Type I life preserver color options (Indian Orange, International Orange, Scarlet Munsell Red) tested elsewhere -- this is a related but separately-defined requirement.
questions.explanation: Per 46 CFR 160.171-9(h), the primary color of the exterior of each immersion suit must be vivid reddish orange (color number 34 of National Bureau of Standards Publication 440) -- a distinct, specifically-defined shade, not simply the life preserver color requirement reused.
questions.sourceReference: Sea-trials batch, Document(6).PDF.pdf (46 CFR 160.171-9(h), Immersion Suits); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-sea-trials-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: An immersion suit's body must be designed to let the wearer jump into the water from what minimum height without injury or damage to the suit?

choices.text: 4.5 meters (about 15 feet)
1 meter (about 3 feet)
choices.misconception: Understates the tested design height.

choices.text: 10 meters (about 33 feet)
choices.misconception: Overstates the tested design height.

choices.text: Immersion suits aren't designed to be jumped in at all
choices.misconception: The suit's construction is specifically tested for exactly this scenario.

hints: This figure, converted to feet, closely matches a jump-height fact taught elsewhere in this course -- but it's a separate, independent design spec, not a restatement of that fact.
questions.explanation: Per 46 CFR 160.171-9(b), an immersion suit's body must be designed to allow the wearer to jump from a height of at least 4.5 m (about 15 feet) into the water without injury and without dislodging or damaging the suit. This closely matches this course's separately-taught 'preferred jump height of 15 feet or less' guidance -- two independent facts (a suit design spec and a general abandon-ship practice) that reinforce rather than duplicate each other.
questions.sourceReference: Sea-trials batch, Document(6).PDF.pdf (46 CFR 160.171-9(b), Immersion Suits); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-sea-trials-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: How much of the wearer's body must an approved immersion suit cover?

choices.text: The entire body, except for the area of the nose and eyes
Only the torso and legs, leaving the arms free
choices.misconception: Understates the required coverage -- the arms are covered too.

choices.text: Everything including the face, with a clear panel over the eyes
choices.misconception: Overstates the coverage -- the nose and eyes remain uncovered, not covered by a clear panel.

choices.text: Only from the waist down
choices.misconception: Drastically understates the required coverage.

hints: Think about what a survivor still needs to be able to do while wearing it: see and breathe freely.
questions.explanation: Per 46 CFR 160.171-9(a), an immersion suit must be designed to cover the wearer's entire body, except for the area of the nose and eyes.
questions.sourceReference: Sea-trials batch, Document(6).PDF.pdf (46 CFR 160.171-9(a), Immersion Suits); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-sea-trials-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: Why must a lifeboat's on-load release mechanism require two separate, deliberate actions by the operator to activate?

choices.text: To prevent the boat from being accidentally released while it's still loaded and suspended from the falls
To make the mechanism slower to use during a genuine emergency
choices.misconception: Reverses the actual safety intent -- the requirement exists to prevent an unwanted release, not to slow down an intended one.

choices.text: To allow two different crew members to physically share the task
choices.misconception: Invents an unstated crew-coordination purpose the regulation doesn't describe.

choices.text: There's no specific reason -- it's an arbitrary design choice
choices.misconception: Dismisses a deliberate, documented safety requirement as arbitrary.

hints: Think about what happens if a fully loaded, suspended lifeboat's hooks release by accident, before the boat is in the water.
questions.explanation: Per 46 CFR 160.133-7(b)(7), the on-load operation of a lifeboat's release mechanism must require two separate, deliberate actions by the operator -- a safeguard against an accidental release while the boat is still loaded and suspended from the falls.
questions.sourceReference: Sea-trials batch, Document(6).PDF.pdf (46 CFR 160.133-7(b)(7), Release Mechanisms for Lifeboats and Rescue Boats); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-sea-trials-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What visibility must a lifeboat's operator have from the control station, per Coast Guard design requirements?

choices.text: A full 360 degrees around the lifeboat, moving their head and torso as needed to see past any obstructions
Only a forward-facing view, like a car's windshield
choices.misconception: Understates the required field of view.

choices.text: Only a rear-facing view, for backing down
choices.misconception: Describes only a fraction of the actual requirement.

choices.text: No specific visibility requirement exists -- it's left entirely to the manufacturer
choices.misconception: Dismisses a real, specific regulatory requirement.

hints: Think about needing to see approaching hazards, other survivors in the water, and the boat's own surroundings all at once.
questions.explanation: Per 46 CFR 160.135-7(b)(2), a lifeboat's operator's station must be designed so the operator, when seated at the control station, has visibility 360 degrees around the lifeboat, with any areas obstructed by the lifeboat's structure or fittings made visible by moving the operator's head and torso.
questions.sourceReference: Sea-trials batch, Document(6).PDF.pdf (46 CFR 160.135-7(b)(2), Lifeboats (SOLAS)); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-sea-trials-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: Besides the person placed in charge of a lifeboat, what other leadership role must be designated for it?

choices.text: A second-in-command
A backup coxswain who only acts if the vessel sinks
choices.misconception: Invents a conditional role not described by the regulation.

choices.text: No other role is required beyond the person in charge
choices.misconception: A second-in-command is specifically required in addition to the person in charge.

choices.text: A designated note-taker to log the voyage
choices.misconception: Invents an unrelated clerical role not part of the actual requirement.

hints: Think about who takes over if the person in charge is incapacitated.
questions.explanation: Per 46 CFR 199.100(d), a second-in-command must be designated for each lifeboat, in addition to the person placed in charge -- and the second-in-command must also have a list of the lifeboat's crew.
questions.sourceReference: Sea-trials batch, Document(7).PDF.pdf (46 CFR 199.100(d), Manning of Survival Craft and Supervision); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-sea-trials-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: A lifeboat or liferaft has a prescribed complement of 25 or more persons. What additional crewing requirement does this trigger?

choices.text: One additional certificated lifeboatman, beyond the person placed in command
A second full crew must be trained and kept on standby
choices.misconception: Overstates the actual requirement.

choices.text: No additional requirement -- capacity alone doesn't affect crewing
choices.misconception: A complement of 25 or more persons specifically does trigger an additional crewing requirement.

choices.text: The vessel must carry a spare lifeboat of equal capacity
choices.misconception: Invents an unrelated equipment requirement, not a crewing one.

hints: Larger capacity means more people to manage during a real emergency -- the crewing requirement scales with it.
questions.explanation: Per 46 CFR 169.819(b), the master must place a licensed deck officer, able seaman, or certificated lifeboatman in command of each lifeboat or liferaft -- and each one with a prescribed complement of 25 or more persons must have one additional certificated lifeboatman.
questions.sourceReference: Sea-trials batch, Document(7).PDF.pdf (46 CFR 169.819(b), Manning of Lifeboats and Liferafts); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-sea-trials-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: How often must each lifeboat actually be lowered into the water (not just swung out), per drill requirements?

choices.text: At least once every 3 months
At least once every 3 years
choices.misconception: Understates how often by a large margin.

choices.text: Only once, during initial vessel certification
choices.misconception: Treats a recurring requirement as a one-time event.

choices.text: Every single week, without exception
choices.misconception: Overstates the frequency -- weekly is the requirement for the fire-and-boat drill itself, which doesn't necessarily put every lifeboat in the water each time.

hints: This is a distinct, less-frequent requirement from the weekly fire-and-boat drill schedule tested elsewhere.
questions.explanation: Per 46 CFR 169.833(b)(5), the master shall ensure that each lifeboat is lowered to the water at least once every 3 months -- distinct from the weekly fire-and-boat-drill requirement, which doesn't necessarily put every lifeboat in the water each time.
questions.sourceReference: Sea-trials batch, Document(7).PDF.pdf (46 CFR 169.833(b)(5), Fire and Boat Drills); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-sea-trials-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: Is a radar reflector always required inside a SOLAS inflatable liferaft?

choices.text: No -- it may be omitted if the outside of the raft's container is marked with a notice saying so
Yes, without exception, on every SOLAS liferaft
choices.misconception: The regulation explicitly allows a marked exception.

choices.text: No, radar reflectors are never permitted inside liferafts
choices.misconception: Reverses the actual rule -- radar reflectors are normally included, with a specific marked exception, not banned outright.

choices.text: Only liferafts under a certain size are exempt
choices.misconception: Invents a size-based exemption that isn't the actual condition -- the exemption depends on the container being marked, not the raft's size.

hints: The exception depends on a label on the container, not on the raft's size or class.
questions.explanation: Per 46 CFR 160.151-21(n), a SOLAS inflatable liferaft's radar reflector may be omitted if the outside of the raft's container includes a notice near the "SOLAS A" or "SOLAS B" marking indicating that no radar reflector is included.
questions.sourceReference: Sea-trials batch, Document(6).PDF.pdf (46 CFR 160.151-21(n), Equipment required for SOLAS A and SOLAS B inflatable liferafts); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-sea-trials-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: Where must a SOLAS inflatable liferaft's heaving line be attached?

choices.text: Near the entrance furthest from the painter attachment
Directly beside the painter attachment
choices.misconception: Reverses the actual required placement.

choices.text: Coiled loose inside the raft, unattached to any point
choices.misconception: The line must be attached at a specific point, not left loose.

choices.text: At the exact center of the raft's floor
choices.misconception: Not the attachment point described by the regulation.

hints: Think about wanting the heaving line accessible from the side of the raft that's farthest from where it's already tethered to the ship or another line.
questions.explanation: Per 46 CFR 160.151-21(a), a SOLAS inflatable liferaft's buoyant heaving line must have a breaking strength of not less than 1.1 kN (250 lb) and must be attached to the raft near the entrance furthest from the painter attachment -- a different fact from the heaving line's overall length, which is tested elsewhere in this course.
questions.sourceReference: Sea-trials batch, Document(6).PDF.pdf (46 CFR 160.151-21(a), Equipment required for SOLAS A and SOLAS B inflatable liferafts); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-sea-trials-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What is the minimum length required for paddles carried in a SOLAS inflatable liferaft?

choices.text: 1.2 meters (about 4 feet)
0.3 meters (about 1 foot)
choices.misconception: Far too short to be a usable paddle.

choices.text: 3 meters (about 10 feet)
choices.misconception: Overstates the actual minimum length.

choices.text: No minimum length is specified
choices.misconception: The regulation specifies a concrete minimum length.

hints: About the height of a tall person's torso and arm combined.
questions.explanation: Per 46 CFR 160.151-21(f), the paddles carried in a SOLAS inflatable liferaft must be at least 1.2 m (4 ft) long.
questions.sourceReference: Sea-trials batch, Document(6).PDF.pdf (46 CFR 160.151-21(f), Equipment required for SOLAS A and SOLAS B inflatable liferafts); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-sea-trials-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What whistle signal is used to STOP lowering a lifeboat that's already being lowered?

choices.text: Two short blasts
One short blast
choices.misconception: One short blast is the signal to START lowering, not stop it.

choices.text: One long blast
choices.misconception: Not the signal used for this specific action.

choices.text: Three short blasts
choices.misconception: Not the signal used for this specific action.

hints: This is the direct complement to the already-taught signal for commencing lowering -- one more blast than that one.
questions.explanation: Per 46 CFR 169.815(c), where whistle signals are used for handling lifeboats, one short blast means lower the lifeboats, and two short blasts means stop lowering them.
questions.sourceReference: Sea-trials batch, Document(7).PDF.pdf (46 CFR 169.815(c)(2), Emergency Signals); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-sea-trials-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: How often must a vessel's EPIRB be tested using its integrated test circuit?

choices.text: Monthly
Weekly
choices.misconception: More frequent than actually required.

choices.text: Annually
choices.misconception: Less frequent than actually required.

choices.text: Only once, at installation
choices.misconception: Treats a recurring test requirement as a one-time event.

hints: This is a routine, recurring self-test, not an annual inspection.
questions.explanation: Per 46 CFR 169.831(a), the master shall ensure the EPIRB is tested monthly, using the integrated test circuit and output indicator, to determine that it is operative.
questions.sourceReference: Sea-trials batch, Document(7).PDF.pdf (46 CFR 169.831(a), Emergency Position Indicating Radio Beacon (EPIRB)); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-sea-trials-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: Besides being conducted weekly, when else must a fire and boat drill be held, per Coast Guard regulation?

choices.text: Within 24 hours of leaving a port, if more than 25 percent of the ship's company was replaced there
Only after an actual emergency has occurred
choices.misconception: Drills are proactive preparation, not something triggered only by a real incident -- and this ignores the actual crew-turnover trigger.

choices.text: Whenever a passenger specifically requests one
choices.misconception: Invents an unstated passenger-driven trigger.

choices.text: There is no additional trigger beyond the weekly schedule
choices.misconception: The crew-turnover trigger is a real, specific additional requirement beyond the weekly schedule.

hints: Think about a large fraction of the crew being new to the vessel all at once -- they haven't drilled on this specific ship yet.
questions.explanation: Per 46 CFR 169.833(a), the master shall conduct a fire and boat drill each week -- and at least one fire and boat drill must be held within 24 hours of leaving a port if more than 25 percent of the ship's company have been replaced at that port.
questions.sourceReference: Sea-trials batch, Document(7).PDF.pdf (46 CFR 169.833(a), Fire and Boat Drills); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-sea-trials-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What is the standard requirement used to determine the length of a lifeboat's sea painter?

choices.text: At least twice the distance between the boat deck and the ship's waterline when the ship is light
A fixed length of 50 feet, regardless of the ship's size
choices.misconception: Sea painter length scales with the ship's freeboard, not a single fixed figure for every vessel.

choices.text: Exactly equal to the distance between the boat deck and the waterline, with no multiplier
choices.misconception: Understates the required length -- the actual requirement is at least twice that distance, not a 1-to-1 match.

choices.text: Half the distance between the boat deck and the waterline
choices.misconception: Inverts the actual ratio -- the painter must be longer than the deck-to-waterline distance, not shorter.

hints: Think about needing enough slack for the boat to reach the water safely even as the ship's freeboard changes with loading.
questions.explanation: A lifeboat's sea painter must be at least twice as long as the distance between the boat deck and the ship's waterline when the ship is light (i.e., at its lightest draft) -- made of manila fiber or an equal-strength synthetic line at least 2 3/4 inches in circumference.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-3, image 2026-07-07_064497-1.jpg (Review Quiz #3, question 2 of 14); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-3-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: A lifeboat's two hatchets are secured by lanyards to each end of the boat. Where specifically, at each end, are they positioned?

choices.text: Near the releasing gear
Inside the sealed equipment locker
choices.misconception: Hatchets must be readily available for use, not sealed away in a locker.

choices.text: Underneath the side benches
choices.misconception: Confuses hatchet stowage with where buoyancy units are typically located.

choices.text: Wherever there's convenient space -- no specific spot is required
choices.misconception: A specific position near the releasing gear is required, not an arbitrary convenient spot.

hints: Think about why a hatchet needs to be close at hand to the gear that could become fouled or need emergency cutting.
questions.explanation: A lifeboat's two hatchets are secured by lanyards to each end of the boat, near the releasing gear -- positioned there so they're immediately at hand if gear becomes entangled or fouled and needs to be cut free.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-3, image 2026-07-07_064497-1.jpg (Review Quiz #3, question 4 of 14); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-3-source-intake.json. Note: the underlying 'one hatchet at each end' fact is already live via q-quiz1a-bow-stern-hatchet (also independently confirmed by 46 CFR 169.529(k)) -- this question deliberately tests only the additional 'near the releasing gear' placement detail, which is new content not covered by that existing question.

questions.prompt: What piece of lifeboat equipment reduces the possibility of the boat capsizing?

choices.text: A sea anchor (drogue)
The boat's compass
choices.misconception: A compass aids navigation, not stability.

choices.text: The bailer
choices.misconception: A bailer removes water that's already inside the boat -- it doesn't prevent capsizing.

choices.text: The releasing gear
choices.misconception: Releasing gear frees the boat from the falls during launch -- unrelated to stability once underway.

hints: Think about what keeps the boat's bow pointed into oncoming seas rather than turning broadside to them.
questions.explanation: A sea anchor (drogue) is lifeboat equipment that reduces the possibility of capsizing, primarily by holding the boat's bow into the seas and slowing unwanted drift.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-3, image 2026-07-07_064497-2.jpg (Review Quiz #3, question 5 of 14); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-3-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: A lifeboat's sea anchor rig includes both a tripping line and a drag (holding) line. What's the difference in what each one does?

choices.text: The tripping line brings the sea anchor's apex forward and empties it of water so it can be retrieved; the drag line is what fills with water and creates drag while the sea anchor is deployed
Both lines do the same thing -- create drag -- just attached at different points
choices.misconception: Collapses two distinct functions -- retrieval/collapsing vs. active drag-creation -- into one.

choices.text: The drag line is used to retrieve and collapse the sea anchor; the tripping line creates the drag while deployed
choices.misconception: Reverses the two lines' actual roles.

choices.text: The tripping line and drag line are two names for the same single line
choices.misconception: Treats two distinct, separately-purposed lines as if they were one.

hints: One of these lines is for bringing the sea anchor IN; the other is for keeping it working WHILE it's out.
To get the most drag from a sea anchor, the line that creates that drag should be towed while slack is paid out.
questions.explanation: A sea anchor's tripping line brings its apex forward, emptying it of water so it can be pulled aboard -- it's the retrieval line. The drag (holding) line is the one that fills the sea anchor's cone with water and creates drag on the lifeboat while it's deployed; towing it with slack paid out creates the most drag.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-3, image 2026-07-07_064497-2.jpg (Review Quiz #3, questions 6 and 9 of 14, combined -- question 6 asked only for the retrieval procedure, question 9 asked for both lines and their uses; merged into one compare-and-contrast question rather than drafted as two overlapping questions on the same tripping-line fact); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-3-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What is the diameter requirement for the sheaves used with davit falls?

choices.text: The diameter at the base of the groove must be at least 12 times the diameter of the wire rope
The diameter at the base of the groove must be at least equal to the diameter of the wire rope
choices.misconception: Understates the required ratio -- a 1-to-1 match would be far too small to protect the wire rope from excessive bending stress.

choices.text: The diameter at the base of the groove must be at least 50 times the diameter of the wire rope
choices.misconception: Overstates the required ratio well beyond the actual requirement.

choices.text: There is no specific diameter requirement -- any sheave that fits the wire rope is acceptable
choices.misconception: A specific minimum ratio is required, not an arbitrary fit.

hints: Think about protecting the wire rope from excessive bending stress and fatigue as it runs over the sheave.
questions.explanation: A davit fall sheave's groove-base diameter must be at least 12 times the diameter of the wire rope running over it -- too small a sheave would over-stress and fatigue the wire.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-3, image 2026-07-07_064497-3.jpg (Review Quiz #3, question 11 of 14); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-3-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: Why is it important to adequately lubricate a lifeboat davit's fall wires?

choices.text: Without lubrication, the wires will corrode internally and lose their flexibility
Lubrication is purely cosmetic and has no protective function
choices.misconception: Dismisses a genuine protective function -- lubrication actively prevents internal corrosion.

choices.text: Lubrication makes the wire rope heavier, which improves lowering speed
choices.misconception: Invents an unrelated, incorrect benefit -- lubrication is about corrosion protection, not weight.

choices.text: It's only necessary in freshwater ports, not at sea
choices.misconception: Invents an unstated freshwater-specific exception -- the corrosion risk applies generally.

hints: Think about what happens to the individual wires bundled inside a wire rope if moisture reaches them and isn't kept out.
questions.explanation: Fall wires must be adequately lubricated because, without it, they'll corrode internally and lose their flexibility -- lubrication protects against that corrosion.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-3, image 2026-07-07_064497-3.jpg (Review Quiz #3, question 12 of 14); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-3-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: When a lifeboat's davit is fitted with a winch, what material and construction are the falls typically made from?

choices.text: Wire rope, 6x19 regular lay, filler wire construction
Manila fiber rope, matching the sea painter's material
choices.misconception: Confuses the falls (which must withstand the mechanical load of a winch) with the sea painter's fiber-rope material.

choices.text: Nylon webbing, for maximum stretch under load
choices.misconception: Excess stretch under load is undesirable for falls -- wire rope is used specifically because it doesn't stretch significantly.

choices.text: Chain, for maximum strength
choices.misconception: Not the actual material used -- chain would be impractical to spool on a winch drum.

hints: Think about what needs to wind cleanly and repeatedly around a winch drum without excessive stretch.
questions.explanation: When a davit has a winch, the falls are made of wire rope -- specifically 6x19 regular lay, filler wire construction.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-3, image 2026-07-07_064497-3.jpg (Review Quiz #3, question 14 of 14); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-3-source-intake.json. CONFIDENCE NOTE: '6x19 regular lay filler wire' is a real, standard wire-rope construction term consistent with the handwriting and used with reasonable confidence, but was not independently cross-verified against a second source the way some other batches' facts have been -- worth a quick confirmation before treating as fully settled.

questions.prompt: On a roller gravity davit, what does the limit switch do?

choices.text: Prevents the lifeboat from being raised all the way up to the davit stops
Automatically starts the engine once the boat is waterborne
choices.misconception: Confuses the limit switch with an unrelated engine-starting function.

choices.text: Sounds an alarm if the boat is lowered too slowly
choices.misconception: Invents an unrelated speed-alarm function -- the limit switch is about over-travel, not speed.

choices.text: Releases the gripes automatically at the start of launching
choices.misconception: Confuses the limit switch with the separate gripes/cradle-securing system.

hints: Think about what stops the boat from being cranked or winched too far in the wrong direction, back up into the davit structure itself.
This fitting is specifically associated with roller gravity davits.
questions.explanation: A limit switch, found on roller gravity davits, prevents the lifeboat from being raised all the way up to the davit stops -- guarding against over-travel damage during recovery.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-4, images 2026-07-07_064994-1.jpg and 2026-07-07_064994-5.jpg (Review Quiz #4, questions 1 and 18 of 24, combined -- question 1 asked which davit type has a limit switch, question 18 asked what it does; merged into one question rather than drafted as two overlapping questions on the same fitting); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-4-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What purpose do a lifeboat's gripes serve?

choices.text: They fasten the lifeboat firmly against the ship's davit frame or cradle when it's in a stowed position
They release the boat cleanly from the falls once it's safely in the water
choices.misconception: Describes the separate hook/releasing-gear system, not the gripes' cradle-securing function.

choices.text: They regulate the lowering speed of a gravity davit
choices.misconception: Confuses gripes with the governor/centrifugal brake, a different fitting entirely.

choices.text: They provide handholds for crew boarding the boat
choices.misconception: Invents an unrelated boarding-aid function.

hints: Think about what keeps the boat from shifting in its cradle while the ship is underway, before any launch begins.
questions.explanation: Lifeboat gripes fasten the boat firmly against the ship's davit frame or cradle while it's in its stowed position -- a separate function from the hook/releasing-gear system that lets the boat go once it's in the water, or the turnbuckle that removes slack from the gripes themselves.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-4, image 2026-07-07_064994-1.jpg (Review Quiz #4, question 2 of 24); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-4-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What mechanism is used to secure an open lifeboat's sea painter to the forward inboard thwart?

choices.text: A toggle pin arrangement
A permanent weld
choices.misconception: A permanently welded painter couldn't be released or replaced -- the attachment must be a releasable fitting.

choices.text: Ordinary knotted rope, with no hardware
choices.misconception: Understates the attachment -- a specific pin fitting is used, not just a knot.

choices.text: The same turnbuckle used to tension the gripes
choices.misconception: Confuses the sea painter's attachment hardware with the separate turnbuckle used on the gripes.

hints: This is a quick-release-style pin fitting, distinct from a permanent or improvised attachment.
questions.explanation: An open lifeboat's sea painter is secured to a strong point on the forward inboard thwart using a toggle pin arrangement.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-4, image 2026-07-07_064994-1.jpg (Review Quiz #4, question 3 of 24); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-4-source-intake.json. Note: the live q-ch2-seapainteruse already tests WHERE the sea painter is secured (the forward one-third of the boat); this question deliberately tests only the toggle-pin attachment mechanism, a distinct new detail.

questions.prompt: What prevents a lifeboat fall's block rings from prematurely falling out of the releasing hooks?

choices.text: A locking cam behind the main hook, plus a hydrostatic interlock
A second, backup set of falls
choices.misconception: Invents an unrelated redundant-rigging solution -- the actual answer is a specific locking/interlock mechanism on the hook itself.

choices.text: Nothing -- the block rings are held in place only by tension on the falls
choices.misconception: Dismisses a real, deliberate safety mechanism -- tension alone isn't the safeguard.

choices.text: The gripes
choices.misconception: Confuses this with the separate gripes system that secures the boat in its stowed cradle, not the hook/block-ring interface.

hints: Two separate safeguards work together here: one purely mechanical, one that only allows release once the boat is in the water.
questions.explanation: A locking cam behind the main hook, combined with a hydrostatic interlock, prevents a lifeboat fall's block rings from prematurely falling out of the releasing hooks.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-4, image 2026-07-07_064994-1.jpg (Review Quiz #4, question 4 of 24); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-4-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: If a ship is pitching and rolling excessively, how should a davit's frapping lines be rigged?

choices.text: Fore and aft
Athwartship (side to side)
choices.misconception: The wrong orientation for controlling excessive pitching and rolling motion.

choices.text: Straight down, vertically
choices.misconception: Doesn't provide the fore-and-aft control needed in a pitching, rolling sea.

choices.text: Frapping lines aren't used in rough weather
choices.misconception: Reverses the actual guidance -- frapping lines matter most, and need a specific rigging orientation, precisely when conditions are rough.

hints: Think about which direction of boat motion (pitching/rolling) needs the most control as the boat is steadied against the ship's side.
questions.explanation: When a ship is pitching and rolling excessively, a davit's frapping lines should be rigged fore and aft, to help steady the lifeboat against the ship's side under those conditions.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-4, image 2026-07-07_064994-2.jpg (Review Quiz #4, question 5 of 24); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-4-source-intake.json. Note: the live q-gloss-frapping already tests the general definition/purpose of frapping lines; this question tests the specific fore-and-aft rigging detail for rough-weather conditions, which that question doesn't cover.

questions.prompt: What is the general sequence of steps for lowering an open lifeboat (classroom model)?

choices.text: Preparing the boat, swinging it out over the ship's side, boarding, then safely releasing it into the water
Boarding, preparing the boat, swinging it out, then releasing it
choices.misconception: Boards before the boat is even prepared or swung out -- out of order.

choices.text: Swinging the boat out, releasing it into the water, then boarding once it's afloat
choices.misconception: Releases the boat into the water before anyone has boarded -- reverses the intended sequence.

choices.text: Preparing the boat, boarding immediately, then swinging it out and releasing it in one motion
choices.misconception: Skips swinging the boat out over the ship's side before boarding begins.

hints: Think about the boat needing to be ready and positioned outboard before anyone climbs in.
questions.explanation: The general sequence for lowering an open lifeboat is: preparing the boat, swinging it out over the ship's side, boarding, and then safely releasing it into the water.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-4, image 2026-07-07_064994-2.jpg (Review Quiz #4, question 6 of 24); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-4-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What piece of equipment regulates the speed at which a gravity davit lowers a lifeboat, and what is the maximum speed?

choices.text: A governor / centrifugal brake, with a maximum speed of 120 feet per minute
The limit switch, with no maximum speed specified
choices.misconception: Confuses the limit switch (which prevents over-raising) with the separate speed-regulating governor/brake.

choices.text: A governor / centrifugal brake, with a maximum speed of 12 feet per minute
choices.misconception: Understates the actual maximum speed by a factor of 10.

choices.text: A hand crank, with the speed controlled entirely by the operator
choices.misconception: A hand crank is used for mechanical davits, not for regulating a gravity davit's automatic lowering speed.

hints: Think about a mechanical device that resists speed increases as it spins faster -- that's the basic principle of a centrifugal brake.
questions.explanation: A governor / centrifugal brake regulates the speed at which a gravity davit lowers a lifeboat, with a maximum speed of 120 feet per minute.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-4, image 2026-07-07_064994-3.jpg (Review Quiz #4, question 8 of 24); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-4-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What is the best procedure for positioning a ship to pick up a lifeboat at sea using the lifeboat's sea painter?

choices.text: Position the ship ahead of and to leeward of the lifeboat, with the wind broad on the bow
Position the ship directly downwind of the lifeboat, letting the wind push the ship toward it
choices.misconception: Ignores the ahead-and-leeward positioning that actually makes pickup safe and controllable.

choices.text: Position the ship astern of the lifeboat and approach from directly behind
choices.misconception: Not the recommended relative position -- the ship should be ahead of the lifeboat, not astern.

choices.text: Position doesn't matter, as long as the sea painter is ready to pass
choices.misconception: Dismisses a real, specific positioning procedure as irrelevant.

hints: Think about keeping the lifeboat in a controlled, predictable position relative to the ship's hull and the wind, rather than letting either drift unpredictably.
questions.explanation: To pick up a lifeboat at sea using its sea painter, the ship should be positioned ahead of and to leeward of the lifeboat, with the wind broad on the bow -- giving controlled, predictable positioning for the pickup.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-4, image 2026-07-07_064994-3.jpg (Review Quiz #4, question 9 of 24); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-4-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What kinds of signals are used when conducting lifeboat launching or recovery operations?

choices.text: A combination of internationally standardized sound signals, visual communication, and verbal commands
Verbal commands only -- sound and visual signals are reserved for distress situations
choices.misconception: Understates the actual signal toolkit used during routine launching/recovery operations.

choices.text: Radio communication only, with no sound or visual signals
choices.misconception: Radio isn't the standard method described -- sound, visual, and verbal signals are all used together.

choices.text: There's no standardized approach -- each crew develops its own signals
choices.misconception: Dismisses the internationally standardized nature of these signals.

hints: Think about redundancy -- if one signal type can't be seen or heard clearly, another type should still get the message across.
questions.explanation: Lifeboat launching and recovery operations rely on a combination of internationally standardized sound signals, visual communication, and verbal commands, rather than any single method alone.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-4, image 2026-07-07_064994-3.jpg (Review Quiz #4, question 10 of 24); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-4-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What must be done prior to using the hand crank on a lifeboat davit's winch?

choices.text: Disable the power / motor
Fully lower the lifeboat first
choices.misconception: Not the actual precaution -- the hand crank can be needed at various stages, not only after full lowering.

choices.text: Remove the fall wires entirely
choices.misconception: The fall wires stay in place -- removing them would defeat the winch's purpose.

choices.text: Nothing -- the hand crank can be used at any time, powered or not
choices.misconception: Ignores a real safety precaution -- using the hand crank while power is still engaged would be hazardous.

hints: Think about what would happen if the motor engaged while someone was manually cranking the same mechanism.
questions.explanation: Power/motor must be disabled prior to using a winch's hand crank -- a precaution against the motor engaging while someone is manually operating the same mechanism.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-4, image 2026-07-07_064994-4.jpg (Review Quiz #4, question 12 of 24); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-4-source-intake.json. Note: the source question also asked WHY this matters, but the student's answer only addressed what must be done -- this question tests only the source-supported half.

questions.prompt: What properties describe the type of wire used to construct lifeboat fall wires?

choices.text: Rotation-resistant and corrosion-resistant steel wire rope
Flexible nylon-coated wire, chosen for its light weight
choices.misconception: Not the actual material -- fall wires are steel wire rope, not a nylon-coated alternative.

choices.text: Plain uncoated steel wire, with no special corrosion treatment
choices.misconception: Understates the actual specification -- corrosion resistance is a deliberate requirement, not absent.

choices.text: Galvanized chain, not wire rope at all
choices.misconception: Confuses fall wires with a chain-based alternative -- falls are wire rope.

hints: Think about two separate stresses a fall wire is under: constant bending/twisting as it runs over sheaves and drums, and ongoing exposure to a marine environment.
questions.explanation: Lifeboat fall wires are rotation-resistant and corrosion-resistant steel wire rope.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-4, image 2026-07-07_064994-4.jpg (Review Quiz #4, question 15 of 24); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-4-source-intake.json. Note: the already-live q-quiz3-winch-falls-material tests the specific wire-rope CONSTRUCTION used for winch-davit falls (6x19 regular lay, filler wire); this question tests the more general rotation-resistant/corrosion-resistant PROPERTIES of lifeboat fall wire generally, a related but distinct angle -- flagged given the topical closeness.

questions.prompt: What type of davit must be hand-cranked to move the lifeboat from its stowed position to the ship's side for lowering?

choices.text: Mechanical davits
Gravity davits
choices.misconception: Gravity davits move the boat to the ship's side using gravity, not hand-cranking.

choices.text: Roller davits
choices.misconception: Not the davit type specifically associated with hand-cranking to reach the ship's side.

choices.text: No davit type requires hand-cranking for this step
choices.misconception: Mechanical davits specifically require hand-cranking for this step.

hints: Think about the davit type that relies on manual effort rather than gravity to get the boat moving toward the ship's side.
questions.explanation: Mechanical davits must be hand-cranked to move the lifeboat from its stowed position to the ship's side for lowering -- unlike gravity davits, which use the boat's own weight for this movement.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-4, image 2026-07-07_064994-5.jpg (Review Quiz #4, question 16 of 24); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-4-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What's the proper way to safely launch a lifeboat when the ship is making way (still moving)?

choices.text: Reduce the vessel's speed, to minimize the effects of propeller wash and drag
Increase the vessel's speed, so the lifeboat clears the hull faster
choices.misconception: Reverses the actual guidance -- more speed means more propeller wash and drag, not less.

choices.text: Speed makes no difference to a safe launch
choices.misconception: Dismisses a real, deliberate precaution -- vessel speed directly affects propeller wash and drag during launch.

choices.text: Turn the ship broadside to minimize the effects of the wind only
choices.misconception: Focuses on wind rather than the actual concern described -- propeller wash and drag from the ship's own movement.

hints: Think about what a moving hull and a turning propeller do to the water right where a lifeboat is being lowered.
questions.explanation: The proper way to safely launch a lifeboat when the ship is making way is to reduce the vessel's speed, minimizing the effects of propeller wash and drag on the lifeboat during launch.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-4, image 2026-07-07_064994-5.jpg (Review Quiz #4, question 17 of 24); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-4-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What type of lifeboat davit is both the most common found on ships today and designed to be operated by one person?

choices.text: The gravity davit
The mechanical davit
choices.misconception: Mechanical davits require hand-cranking, not the simpler one-person operation described here.

choices.text: The roller davit
choices.misconception: Not the davit type this specific combination of facts describes.

choices.text: No single davit type is designed for one-person operation
choices.misconception: The gravity davit specifically is designed for exactly this.

hints: Think about the davit type that uses the lifeboat's own weight to do most of the work, requiring less manual effort from the operator.
questions.explanation: The gravity davit is both the most common and easily operated lifeboat davit type found on ships today, and it's specifically designed to be operated by one person.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-4, images 2026-07-07_064994-4.jpg and 2026-07-07_064994-5.jpg (Review Quiz #4, questions 14 and 19 of 24, combined -- both ask about the same davit type from complementary angles; merged into one question rather than drafted as two overlapping questions); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-4-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What should be done with a lifeboat's magnetic compass when it's not in use?

choices.text: Removed from its mounting bracket and securely stowed in its designated storage box inside the lifeboat
Left mounted in place at all times, whether in use or not
choices.misconception: Leaves the compass mounted and exposed unnecessarily when the boat isn't in use.

choices.text: Left mounted, but covered with a waterproof bag
choices.misconception: Understates the actual requirement -- the compass must be removed and stowed, not just covered in place.

choices.text: Given to the coxswain to keep in a personal locker
choices.misconception: Invents an informal storage arrangement rather than the designated storage box inside the lifeboat itself.

hints: Think about protecting the compass from damage and keeping it out of the way when the boat isn't actively in use.
questions.explanation: A lifeboat's magnetic compass, when not in use, should be removed from its mounting bracket and securely stowed in its designated storage box inside the lifeboat.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-4, image 2026-07-07_064994-6.jpg (Review Quiz #4, question 23 of 24); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-4-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: If there are a number of liferafts scattered about after their ship sinks, what should be done?

choices.text: Group them up and tie them together
Keep them spread apart to cover more search area
choices.misconception: Spreading rafts apart makes each one individually harder to spot and reduces survivors' ability to share resources and support each other.

choices.text: Each raft should deploy its own sea anchor and drift independently
choices.misconception: Ignores the value of grouping rafts together for visibility and mutual aid.

choices.text: Nothing -- rafts should be left exactly as they land in the water
choices.misconception: Passively leaving rafts scattered misses a real, actionable step survivors can take.

hints: Think about visibility to searchers and the ability of survivors in different rafts to help each other.
questions.explanation: If a number of liferafts are scattered about after their ship sinks, survivors should group them up and tie them together -- improving visibility to search and rescue and allowing mutual support between rafts.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-6, image 2026-07-07_065906-1.jpg (Review Quiz #6, question 1 of 22); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-6-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: After landing on a beach with your liferaft, what should you do with the liferaft?

choices.text: Carry it past the high tide line
Leave it at the waterline for easy relaunching if needed
choices.misconception: A rising tide could carry the raft back out to sea or damage it against the shore if left near the waterline.

choices.text: Deflate it immediately and discard it
choices.misconception: The raft remains valuable shelter and equipment after landing -- it shouldn't be discarded.

choices.text: Push it back out to sea once everyone is ashore
choices.misconception: The raft is still useful survival equipment on land and shouldn't be sent back out to sea.

hints: Think about what the tide will do to anything left too close to the water's edge.
questions.explanation: After landing a liferaft on a beach, it should be carried past the high tide line -- protecting it (and its continued usefulness as shelter and equipment) from being carried off or damaged by a rising tide.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-6, image 2026-07-07_065906-1.jpg (Review Quiz #6, question 2 of 22); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-6-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: A liferaft's setup should differ depending on climate. What's the difference between the proper setup in higher (colder) latitudes versus tropical waters?

choices.text: In higher latitudes, inflate the floor and close the doors to lessen cold water radiation; in tropical waters, open the canopy entryways and deflate the floor for ventilation
The setup is identical regardless of climate -- floor and doors are never adjusted
choices.misconception: Ignores that climate-specific adjustments to the floor and doors are a real, deliberate survival practice.

choices.text: In higher latitudes, open the doors for ventilation; in tropical waters, inflate the floor for insulation
choices.misconception: Reverses the two climate-specific setups.

choices.text: The floor should always be deflated and the doors always closed, regardless of climate
choices.misconception: Applies one climate's setup universally, ignoring the need to adapt to conditions.

hints: Think about which direction you want heat to move in each climate -- keeping cold out in higher latitudes, versus letting heat and humidity escape in the tropics.
questions.explanation: In higher (colder) latitudes, inflating the floor and closing the doors lessens cold water radiation into the raft. In tropical waters, the opposite adjustments help: opening the canopy entryways and deflating the floor improve ventilation and reduce heat/humidity buildup.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-6, image 2026-07-07_065906-1.jpg (Review Quiz #6, questions 3 and 4 of 22, combined -- a natural compare-and-contrast pairing of cold-climate and tropical-climate liferaft setup, merged into one question rather than drafted as two separate ones); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-6-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: Besides basic flotation, what items should a lifejacket or personal flotation device have on it?

choices.text: A light, a whistle, reflective patches, and the name of the vessel
A GPS transponder and a two-way radio
choices.misconception: Overstates what's actually required -- these are far more elaborate than the standard items on a PFD.

choices.text: Only a whistle -- no light or reflective material is required
choices.misconception: Understates the required items -- a light and reflective material are also required, not just a whistle.

choices.text: Nothing beyond the flotation material itself

hints: Think about what helps rescuers both see and hear a survivor, plus what helps identify which vessel they came from.
questions.explanation: A lifejacket or PFD should have a light, a whistle, reflective patches, and the name of the vessel on it -- helping rescuers locate, hear, and identify survivors.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-6, image 2026-07-07_065906-1.jpg (Review Quiz #6, question 5 of 22); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-6-source-intake.json. Note: the already-live q-quiz1a-typeI-pfd-false lists 'reflective tape, a whistle, and strobe light' as a true statement about Type I life preservers in passing (as a distractor's descriptive text, not that question's tested answer) -- this question directly tests the fuller item list as its own fact, adding 'name of vessel' as new content.

questions.prompt: What is the safest method of entering the water from a sinking ship if there is an oil fire on the surface?

choices.text: Enter from the stern or bow, on the windward side
Enter from amidships, on the leeward side
choices.misconception: The leeward side is where wind would blow burning oil and smoke toward the survivor -- the opposite of safe.

choices.text: Enter from wherever is most convenient -- fire location doesn't affect the choice
choices.misconception: Dismisses a real, deliberate safety consideration -- the fire's position relative to wind direction matters a great deal.

choices.text: Wait until the fire burns out before entering the water at all
choices.misconception: Waiting isn't a safe option when abandoning a sinking ship -- entry point and side matter, not delay.

hints: Think about which side of the ship the wind would blow burning oil and smoke away from.
questions.explanation: The safest way to enter the water from a sinking ship with an oil fire on the surface is from the stern or bow, on the windward side -- keeping the wind blowing the fire and smoke away from the survivor rather than toward them.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-6, image 2026-07-07_065906-2.jpg (Review Quiz #6, question 10 of 22); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-6-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: When trying to swim to a shore where there is a strong current, what angle should you swim relative to the current?

choices.text: 45 degrees out of (across) the current
Straight against the current, head-on
choices.misconception: Fighting a strong current head-on wastes energy rather than working with it at an angle.

choices.text: Directly with the current, ignoring the shore's direction
choices.misconception: Swimming purely with the current abandons the goal of actually reaching shore.

choices.text: 90 degrees, directly across the current with no angle toward shore
choices.misconception: A full 90-degree crossing doesn't make progress toward shore -- a 45-degree angle balances crossing the current with heading toward land.

hints: Think about balancing two goals at once: not exhausting yourself fighting the current, and still making progress toward shore.
questions.explanation: When swimming to shore against a strong current, swim at a 45-degree angle out of (relative to) the current rather than straight against it -- conserving energy while still making progress toward land.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-6, image 2026-07-07_065906-3.jpg (Review Quiz #6, question 11 of 22); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-6-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What steps should be taken to maneuver a lifeboat and make way while avoiding broaching in heavy seas?

choices.text: Keep the bow to the seas, and use a sea anchor if necessary
Keep the stern to the seas, running directly downwind
choices.misconception: Running with the stern to the seas increases the risk of broaching, not avoids it.

choices.text: Turn broadside to the seas for a more stable ride
choices.misconception: Being broadside to heavy seas is precisely the broaching risk this question is asking how to avoid.

choices.text: Maintain maximum speed regardless of the boat's heading relative to the seas
choices.misconception: Speed alone doesn't prevent broaching -- heading relative to the seas, and using a sea anchor if needed, is what matters.

hints: Broaching happens when a boat turns unexpectedly broadside to the seas -- think about which heading avoids that.
questions.explanation: To maneuver a lifeboat and make way while avoiding broaching in heavy seas, keep the bow to the seas, and use a sea anchor if necessary to help maintain that heading.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-6, image 2026-07-07_065906-3.jpg (Review Quiz #6, question 12 of 22); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-6-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of collecting condensation in a liferaft, and how is it typically done?

choices.text: It's collected -- with a sponge or a t-shirt -- as a source of drinking water
It's collected to keep the raft's interior dry, then discarded
choices.misconception: Discards the actual value of the collected condensation -- it's kept and used as drinking water, not thrown away.

choices.text: It's collected only to test whether the canopy has a leak
choices.misconception: Invents an unrelated diagnostic purpose -- the real purpose is obtaining drinking water.

choices.text: Condensation isn't worth collecting in a liferaft

hints: Think about how scarce fresh water is in a liferaft, and how even small amounts matter.
questions.explanation: Condensation inside a liferaft is collected -- using a sponge or a t-shirt to soak it up -- as a supplementary source of drinking water, since fresh water is scarce for survivors.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-6, image 2026-07-07_065906-3.jpg and 2026-07-07_065906-4.jpg (Review Quiz #6, questions 13 and 16 of 22, combined -- question 13 asked the purpose, question 16 asked the method with an additional tool; merged into one question rather than drafted as two overlapping questions); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-6-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: Your ship is on course 090°T and someone falls overboard on the starboard side. What maneuver does the mate on watch execute, and what's the ship's heading after its first phase?

choices.text: A Williamson Turn -- turning toward the side the person fell until the heading changes 60 degrees from the original course, reaching 150°T
A Williamson Turn -- turning away from the side the person fell, reaching 030°T
choices.misconception: Turns away from (rather than toward) the side the person fell, and applies the heading change in the wrong direction.

choices.text: An immediate full stop, with no turn at all
choices.misconception: A full stop alone doesn't reposition the ship to recover the person -- the Williamson Turn's heading change is the actual maneuver.

choices.text: A Williamson Turn, but the heading change is 180 degrees immediately, with no intermediate phase

hints: The Williamson Turn's first phase changes the ship's heading by a specific number of degrees from its original course, toward the side the person fell -- not straight to the reciprocal course.
questions.explanation: For a man overboard, the mate on watch executes a Williamson Turn -- turning toward the side the person fell (starboard, here) until the ship's heading has changed 60 degrees from its original course: 090°T + 60° = 150°T. (The full maneuver continues from there to reach the reciprocal heading, retracing the ship's track back to the person's position, but this question covers the well-documented first phase.)
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-6, image 2026-07-07_065906-3.jpg (Review Quiz #6, question 14 of 22); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-6-source-intake.json. Note: the student's answer ('Williamson turn 60°' / '90 = 150°') documents only the turn's first phase; this question is scoped to match exactly what the source supports, not the full maneuver's later steps.

questions.prompt: What is the proper procedure for jumping into the water when abandoning ship?

choices.text: Cross your arms and legs, and make sure there's no debris below before jumping
Keep your arms and legs spread wide for a bigger splash landing
choices.misconception: A wide, spread-limb landing increases the risk of injury on impact -- crossing the arms and legs is the safer technique.

choices.text: Dive in headfirst to minimize impact
choices.misconception: A headfirst dive risks serious head/neck injury, especially into unknown water conditions below.

choices.text: Jump immediately without checking what's below, to save time

hints: Think about protecting your limbs on impact, and checking for hazards in the water below before you commit to jumping.
questions.explanation: When jumping into the water to abandon ship, cross your arms and legs to protect them on impact, and make sure there's no debris (or people) below before jumping.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-6, image 2026-07-07_065906-4.jpg (Review Quiz #6, question 15 of 22); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-6-source-intake.json. Note: distinct from the already-live 'preferred jump height of 15 feet or less' fact -- this question covers body position and a pre-jump safety check, not height.

questions.prompt: What does a continuous ringing of the general alarm bell (no less than 10 seconds), combined with a continuous blast of the ship's whistle (more than 10 seconds), indicate?

choices.text: Fire or a general emergency
Commence lowering the lifeboats
choices.misconception: The lowering signal is a single short blast, not a continuous bell-and-whistle combination.

choices.text: Man overboard
choices.misconception: Not the signal associated with this specific continuous bell-and-whistle combination.

choices.text: Boat drill dismissal
choices.misconception: Dismissal from boat stations uses a different signal (three short blasts of the whistle), not this continuous combination.

hints: This is the ship's first/general alarm -- distinct from the separate signal used specifically to call the crew to boat stations.
questions.explanation: A continuous ringing of the general alarm bell for no less than 10 seconds, combined with a continuous blast of the ship's whistle for more than 10 seconds, indicates fire or a general emergency -- the ship's first alarm signal, distinct from the separate seven-short-blasts-plus-one-long signal used specifically to call the crew to boat stations.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-6, image 2026-07-07_065906-4.jpg (Review Quiz #6, question 17 of 22); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-6-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What oar command is given to take all the way off a lifeboat that's moving through the water?

choices.text: "Hold water all"
"Way enough"
choices.misconception: 'Way enough' is the command to finish the stroke and lift the oars from the rowlocks -- a different action from stopping the boat's way through the water.

choices.text: "Backwater all"
choices.misconception: 'Backwater all' reverses the oar stroke to row backward, rather than bracing the oars to stop the boat's forward way.

choices.text: "Out oars"
choices.misconception: 'Out oars' moves the oars from the side benches to the gunwales, an entirely different action from stopping the boat.

hints: This command braces the oar blades in the water to act as a brake, distinct from the commands that reposition or stow the oars.
questions.explanation: "Hold water all" is the oar command given to take all the way off a lifeboat that's moving through the water -- distinct from 'Way enough' (finishing the stroke and stowing oars), 'Backwater all' (reversing), and 'Out oars' (repositioning oars from the side benches to the gunwales).
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-6, image 2026-07-07_065906-5.jpg (Review Quiz #6, question 21 of 22); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-6-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What is AMVER, and what is its purpose during a search and rescue?

choices.text: An automated system that tracks nearby merchant vessels' positions so the Coast Guard can redirect ships to a disaster scene
A distress beacon carried aboard every lifeboat
choices.misconception: Confuses AMVER (a vessel-tracking and coordination system) with a personal or craft-carried distress beacon like an EPIRB.

choices.text: A rule requiring lifeboats to carry a minimum amount of stowed water
choices.misconception: Confuses AMVER with an equipment-stowage requirement, an unrelated topic.
