choices.text: A radio frequency reserved exclusively for Mayday calls
choices.misconception: Invents a frequency-reservation rule that isn't what AMVER actually is.

hints: Think about how the Coast Guard finds a nearby ship to redirect to a disaster scene it doesn't have its own rescue unit near.
questions.explanation: AMVER (Automated Merchant Vessel Report) is a system that tracks the positions of merchant vessels making offshore voyages, so the Coast Guard can identify and redirect nearby ships to a disaster scene when its own rescue units aren't close by.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Understanding Coast Guard Searches section (manual page ~89); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: In a normal abandon-ship situation, why does survival guidance recommend streaming a sea anchor and drifting with the current, rather than trying to row or sail away under your own power?

choices.text: Staying near the last reported position maximizes rescue chances, since a motorized lifeboat has only about 24 hours of fuel, rowing is slow and exhausting, and the standing lug sail rig is a poor sailing rig
Regulations forbid survival craft from moving under their own power
choices.misconception: Invents a blanket prohibition rather than the actual practical reasoning about rescue-search efficiency and limited propulsion resources.

choices.text: Sea anchors are required to be deployed at all times regardless of the situation
choices.misconception: Overstates the guidance as an absolute rule rather than situational reasoning tied to maximizing rescue chances.

choices.text: Moving away from the last position always improves the odds of finding land faster
choices.misconception: Reverses the actual guidance, which favors staying near the known search area over trying to self-navigate to land.

hints: Consider how a search is organized around a last-known position, and how much fuel/energy a survival craft actually has for self-propulsion.
questions.explanation: Search efforts are organized around a vessel's last reported position, so staying near it maximizes rescue chances. Attempting to move under your own power is a poor alternative: a motorized lifeboat carries only about 24 hours of fuel at 6 knots, rowing is slow and energy-costly, and the standing lug sail rig is a poor sailing rig.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Understanding Coast Guard Searches section (manual page ~89); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: What color is a PFD (life jacket) required to be?

choices.text: A highly visible color such as Indian orange, International orange, or Munsell
Vivid reddish orange, the same color required for immersion suits
choices.misconception: Merges the PFD color requirement with the separate, specifically-named color requirement for immersion suits -- two different pieces of equipment with their own named color standards.

choices.text: Any bright color is acceptable, as long as it isn't black or dark blue
choices.misconception: Understates the requirement as a loose 'any bright color' rule rather than a specific named set of colors.

choices.text: White, to match international distress-signal conventions
choices.misconception: Confuses PFD color requirements with an unrelated distress-signal convention.

hints: This is a named set of specific colors, not just 'bright orange' in general -- and it's a different named requirement from the immersion suit's own color rule.
questions.explanation: A PFD is required to be a highly visible color such as Indian orange, International orange, or Munsell -- a specific, named color requirement. This is a separate rule from the immersion suit's own required color, which is specifically 'vivid reddish orange' -- don't merge the two pieces of equipment's separate color requirements.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Abandoning Ship / PFDs section (manual page ~91); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: Which best distinguishes a PFD (life jacket) from an immersion suit in terms of self-righting an unconscious wearer?

choices.text: A PFD is designed to rapidly turn an unconscious wearer face-up; an immersion suit may not self-right an unconscious wearer the same way
Both a PFD and an immersion suit are designed to self-right an unconscious wearer identically
choices.misconception: Treats the two pieces of equipment as functionally identical on this point, when the manual draws a clear distinction between them.

choices.text: Neither a PFD nor an immersion suit is designed to self-right an unconscious wearer
choices.misconception: Understates the PFD's actual designed self-righting function.

choices.text: An immersion suit self-rights an unconscious wearer, but a PFD does not
choices.misconception: Reverses which piece of equipment actually has the self-righting design feature.

hints: One of these two pieces of equipment is specifically designed to flip an unconscious wearer face-up; the other is not.
questions.explanation: A PFD is designed to rapidly turn an unconscious wearer face-up. An immersion suit, by contrast, may not self-right an unconscious wearer the same way -- a real, testable difference between the two pieces of equipment, not just a stylistic one.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Abandoning Ship / PFDs and Immersion Suits sections (manual pages ~91-92); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: Why might wearing a PFD together with an immersion suit be inappropriate?

choices.text: Most immersion suits have an integrated, orally-inflated buoyancy pillow, and a PFD worn with the suit may obstruct it
Wearing both is always dangerous and never appropriate under any circumstances
choices.misconception: Overstates the guidance -- if the suit lacks the buoyancy pillow, wearing a PFD with it is fine.

choices.text: A PFD and an immersion suit provide identical protection, so combining them wastes equipment
choices.misconception: Misstates the actual concern, which is about physical obstruction of the suit's buoyancy pillow, not redundant protection.

choices.text: Immersion suits are not buoyant on their own and require a PFD to float at all
choices.misconception: Immersion suits are themselves designed to provide flotation; they don't require a PFD to float.

hints: Think about what a PFD worn on the outside of a suit might physically block.
questions.explanation: Most immersion suits have an integrated buoyancy pillow that's inflated by mouth. A PFD worn on top of the suit may obstruct that pillow -- unless the specific suit lacks the pillow, in which case wearing a PFD with it is fine.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Immersion Suits section (manual page ~92); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: What is the key tradeoff of an Anti-Exposure Suit compared to an immersion suit, and how is it typically worn?

choices.text: It trades some of the immersion suit's protection for much better mobility and dexterity, and is meant to be worn together with a lifejacket or buoyant work vest
It provides more thermal protection than an immersion suit and is always worn alone, without a lifejacket
choices.misconception: Reverses the actual tradeoff -- the Anti-Exposure Suit provides less protection in exchange for better mobility, and is meant to be worn with a lifejacket or work vest, not alone.

choices.text: It is functionally identical to an immersion suit and worn the same way
choices.misconception: Treats the two suits as interchangeable, ignoring the manual's explicit distinction between them.

choices.text: It is designed exclusively for use by passengers, not crew
choices.misconception: Invents a passenger-only restriction; the manual describes it as commonly used by rescue boat crews.

hints: Think about who needs to still use their hands and feet freely while wearing exposure protection.
questions.explanation: An Anti-Exposure Suit trades some of an immersion suit's protection for much better mobility and dexterity, and unlike most immersion suits, it's meant to be worn together with a lifejacket or buoyant work vest. It's commonly used by rescue boat crews, who need their hands and feet free.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Anti-Exposure Suit section (manual page ~93); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: Beyond what general jump height, when abandoning ship, does the manual state you will probably injure yourself?

choices.text: Higher than 30 feet
Higher than 15 feet
choices.misconception: Confuses the general injury-risk upper bound with the separate 'below fifteen feet is ideal' preferred-height guidance.

choices.text: Higher than 4.5 meters
choices.misconception: Confuses this general jump-height guidance with the immersion suit's own separate design/performance specification.

choices.text: There is no meaningful upper limit as long as you land feet-first
choices.misconception: Ignores the real injury risk from jumping too far; the manual gives a specific height beyond which injury is likely, not an unlimited safe range.

hints: This is a different, higher figure than the 'ideal' preferred jump height.
questions.explanation: The manual states that jumping from higher than 30 feet will probably cause injury. This is a separate figure from the preferred jump height (below 15 feet is ideal) and from the immersion suit's own 4.5-meter design/performance specification -- three related but distinct numbers.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Entering the Water from a Height section (manual page ~93); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: How should you protect your airway from wave slap while swimming for survival?

choices.text: Cup your hands over your nose and mouth
Hold your breath continuously and avoid breathing until reaching calm water
choices.misconception: Not a realistic or described technique -- the actual guidance is a simple hand-cupping technique, not indefinite breath-holding.

choices.text: Turn onto your back and float motionless until the waves pass
choices.misconception: Describes a general floating technique, not the specific wave-slap airway protection method.

choices.text: Swim underwater continuously to avoid wave slap entirely
choices.misconception: Overstates the response -- the guidance is a simple hand technique, not continuous underwater swimming.

hints: This is a simple hand technique, not a whole-body maneuver.
questions.explanation: Cupping your hands over your nose and mouth protects your airway from wave slap while swimming for survival.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Swimming for Survival section (manual page ~95); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: When swimming through surf, how should you generally handle small waves close to shore versus large waves?

choices.text: Small waves can be body-surfed; large waves should be swum under, between crests, covering your nose and mouth and holding your breath
Both small and large waves should always be swum under to avoid impact
choices.misconception: Overgeneralizes the large-wave technique to small waves as well, when small waves can simply be body-surfed.

choices.text: Both small and large waves should be body-surfed for the fastest approach to shore
choices.misconception: Applies the small-wave technique to large waves too, ignoring the added risk large waves pose.

choices.text: Wave size makes no difference to swimming technique
choices.misconception: Ignores the real distinction the manual draws between how to handle small waves versus large ones.

hints: Think about why a large breaking wave calls for a different response than a small one.
questions.explanation: Small waves close to shore can be body-surfed. Large waves should instead be swum under, between crests, covering your nose and mouth and holding your breath as they pass over you.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Swimming for Survival section (manual page ~95); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: If you must swim through burning oil or debris on the water's surface, what technique should you use once you're in it?

choices.text: Remove your life jacket and shoes, swim underwater as much as possible, and when surfacing, beat the oil away from your face with circular arm motions while turning your back to the flame before diving again
Keep your life jacket on at all times and swim on the surface to stay visible
choices.misconception: Reverses the guidance -- the life jacket and shoes should be removed, and swimming should be underwater as much as possible, not on the surface.

choices.text: Remove all clothing, including your regular clothes, before entering the burning oil
choices.misconception: Overstates the guidance -- only the life jacket and shoes are removed; other clothing is kept on for protection.

choices.text: Face the flame directly while thrashing to clear the oil away
choices.misconception: Facing the flame directly while thrashing is the opposite of the described technique, which is to turn your back to the flame and dive under again.

hints: This is about what to do once you're already swimming through oil, not about which side of the ship to enter from.
questions.explanation: Once swimming through burning oil or debris, remove your life jacket and shoes (keeping other clothing on for protection), swim underwater as much as possible, and when you need to surface, beat the oil away from your face with circular arm motions while turning your back to the flame before diving under again.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Swimming Through Burning Oil section (manual page ~96); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md. Note: distinct from the already-live entry-point question (windward bow/stern entry), which covers a different, earlier phase of the same overall scenario.

questions.prompt: If you find yourself in the water with no life jacket, what are two methods you can use to stay afloat?

choices.text: Using trapped air in your clothing while lying on your back, or a back-float technique that alternates a short breath with sinking slightly and rising again
Swimming continuously in place to generate buoyancy
choices.misconception: Continuous swimming wastes energy rather than conserving it, which is the opposite of the recommended flotation methods.

choices.text: Removing all clothing immediately to reduce drag
choices.misconception: Reverses the guidance -- clothing (if not weighing you down) can trap useful air and should generally be kept on, not removed.

choices.text: Treading water continuously at full effort until rescue arrives
choices.misconception: Describes an exhausting, unsustainable approach rather than the energy-conserving flotation methods actually described.

hints: One method uses air trapped in your clothes; the other is a relaxed floating rhythm that doesn't require staying fully surfaced.
questions.explanation: Two methods for staying afloat without a life jacket: using air trapped in your clothing while lying on your back with your head tilted so your nose and chin stay above water, and a back-float technique where you take a short breath, let yourself sink a little, then rise again -- rather than trying to stay fully surfaced continuously.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Floating With No Life Jacket section (manual pages ~96-97); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: How can a shirt be used to help you stay afloat if you have no life jacket?

choices.text: Blow air into it to trap a bubble at the back, then button the collar tight to hold the air in
Wrap it tightly around your chest to compress your lungs and increase buoyancy
choices.misconception: Describes a technique that would restrict breathing rather than the actual trapped-air method.

choices.text: Soak it in water first so it becomes heavier and acts as a sea anchor
choices.misconception: Describes the opposite effect of what's needed -- the goal is trapping air for buoyancy, not adding weight.

choices.text: Tie the sleeves together and use it as a signaling flag instead of a flotation aid
choices.misconception: Describes using the shirt as a signaling device instead of the actual trapped-air flotation technique.

hints: Think about how you'd trap air inside the shirt, and what keeps that air from just escaping again.
questions.explanation: To use a shirt for flotation: blow air between the buttons to trap a bubble of air at the back of the shirt, then button the collar tight so the trapped air doesn't escape, take a deep breath, and bend your head forward.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Clothing for flotation section (manual page ~98); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: Which of the following is NOT one of the commonly cited reasons seafarers go overboard?

choices.text: Deliberately jumping overboard as part of a scheduled drill
Losing footing on deck or being swept overboard by waves
choices.misconception: This IS one of the cited reasons, not the correct 'NOT' answer.

choices.text: Climbing on or sitting on the ship's railings
choices.misconception: This IS one of the cited reasons, not the correct 'NOT' answer.

choices.text: Being under the influence of alcohol or drugs
choices.misconception: This IS one of the cited reasons, not the correct 'NOT' answer.

hints: Three of these four are accident/behavior-related causes actually listed in the manual; one describes something else entirely.
questions.explanation: The manual's cited reasons seafarers go overboard include accidents (losing footing, being swept by waves, being pulled by mooring lines, falling from an accommodation ladder), climbing or sitting on railings, being under the influence of alcohol or drugs, and working in low visibility or rough sea conditions -- not a deliberate, scheduled drill jump.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Man Overboard section (manual page ~99); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: What should the crew on deck do immediately after someone falls overboard?

choices.text: Throw ring buoys with lifelines toward the person (lighted at night, with a smoke signal by day), hail "Man Overboard, Port (or Starboard) Side" to the wheelhouse, and have a lookout keep continuous eyes on the person's position
Wait for the officer on watch to personally confirm the situation before taking any action
choices.misconception: Describes unnecessary delay rather than the immediate response actions the crew should take right away.

choices.text: Immediately launch a lifeboat before doing anything else
choices.misconception: Skips the immediate priorities (alerting the wheelhouse, keeping the person in sight, throwing flotation) in favor of a slower, more involved response that comes later.

choices.text: Sound the general alarm and have all crew muster at their lifeboat stations
choices.misconception: Sounding the general alarm for a full muster is a different, broader ship's emergency signal, not the specific immediate man-overboard response actions described.

hints: Think about the fastest ways to (1) get the bridge informed, (2) give the person something to hold onto, and (3) not lose sight of them.
questions.explanation: The immediate crew response is to throw ring buoys with lifelines toward the person (a light at night, a smoke signal by day), hail "Man Overboard, Port (or Starboard) Side" to the wheelhouse, and have a lookout keep continuous, uninterrupted eyes on and point at the person's position.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Man Overboard section (manual pages ~99-100); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: Why does the mate on watch turn the ship's stern away from a person who has fallen overboard?

choices.text: To lessen the danger of the vessel's propeller striking the person in the water
To create more visible wake for search aircraft to spot
choices.misconception: Invents an unrelated signaling purpose for the maneuver, when the actual reason is propeller-strike safety.

choices.text: To reduce engine noise near the person for easier communication
choices.misconception: Invents an unrelated noise-reduction purpose; the actual reason concerns physical safety from the propeller.

choices.text: It's simply standard procedure with no specific safety reason
choices.misconception: Dismisses a real, specific safety reason for the maneuver -- it is done deliberately to protect the person from the propeller, not as an arbitrary habit.

hints: Think about which part of the ship poses the most direct physical danger to someone in the water nearby.
questions.explanation: The mate on watch turns the ship's stern away from a person who has fallen overboard specifically to lessen the danger of the vessel's propeller striking them.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Man Overboard section (manual page ~100); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: During a Williamson Turn, why does the ship first turn the rudder hard over toward the side the person fell from, rather than away from it?

choices.text: It reduces the chance of the vessel's propeller striking the person who fell overboard
It's simply the fastest way to reach full reverse speed
choices.misconception: Invents a speed-related reasoning unconnected to the actual propeller-safety purpose of the initial turn direction.

choices.text: It has no specific safety purpose -- either direction would work equally well
choices.misconception: Dismisses a real, deliberate safety reason for choosing that specific turn direction.

choices.text: It positions the ship to deploy a rescue boat from the opposite side
choices.misconception: Rescue-boat deployment side is not the reason given for the turn's initial direction; the stated reason is propeller-strike safety for the person in the water.

hints: This is the same underlying safety reason as turning the stern away from the person -- both protect them from the same hazard.
questions.explanation: Turning the rudder hard over toward the side the person fell from -- the first phase of a Williamson Turn -- reduces the chance of the vessel's propeller striking them, the same underlying safety concern as turning the stern away from the person in the initial response.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Man Overboard section (manual page ~100); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md. Note: deliberately does not use the source's degree-by-degree worked example, which the source intake flagged as internally inconsistent (its stated arithmetic doesn't check out). Only the turn's directional purpose is tested here.

questions.prompt: The manual gives an air-temperature range associated with hypothermia risk. What is that range, and how does it differ from the water-temperature threshold you may already know?

choices.text: Roughly 30-50°F for air temperature -- a separate figure from the 70°F water-temperature threshold at which the average person can't generate enough heat to keep up with losses while immersed
The same 70°F figure applies to both air and water temperature
choices.misconception: Merges two distinct figures for two different exposure types (air vs. water immersion) into one number.

choices.text: Roughly 30-50°F for both air and water temperature
choices.misconception: States a real air-temperature range, but incorrectly claims it also applies to water temperature, when the source gives a separate, different water-immersion figure (70 degrees Fahrenheit).

choices.text: The manual gives no air-temperature figure, only a water-temperature one
choices.misconception: Understates the source, which gives both an air-temperature range and a separate water-immersion threshold.

hints: These are two different figures for two different situations -- being in cold air versus being immersed in cold water.
questions.explanation: The manual gives roughly 30-50°F as the air-temperature range associated with hypothermia risk -- a separate figure from the water-immersion threshold of 70°F, at or below which the average person can't generate body heat fast enough to keep up with how quickly they're losing it in water. Don't merge the two: one is about air exposure, the other about water immersion.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Cold Water Survival / Hypothermia section (manual page ~101); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: Does alcohol help protect against hypothermia in cold-water survival situations?

choices.text: No -- alcohol does not prevent hypothermia and actually increases the risk
Yes, in small amounts, because it creates a warming sensation that reflects real heat generation
choices.misconception: Mistakes alcohol's warming SENSATION (caused by blood vessels dilating near the skin) for an actual protective effect -- it's the opposite, since it accelerates heat loss.

choices.text: Only if the survivor is also shivering violently
choices.misconception: Shivering intensity isn't described as a precondition for alcohol's effect on hypothermia risk -- alcohol increases the risk regardless.

choices.text: Yes, but only for a person who is not already wet
choices.misconception: Being dry isn't described as a precondition either -- alcohol's effect on hypothermia risk isn't tied to whether the person is wet.

hints: Alcohol can make you FEEL warmer even as it actually works against your body's cold defenses -- don't confuse the sensation with the real effect.
questions.explanation: Alcohol does not prevent hypothermia -- it actually increases the risk, despite sometimes producing a warming sensation. This is a common misconception worth remembering explicitly.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Cold Water Survival / Hypothermia section (manual page ~101); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: Roughly how long can a good swimmer last swimming in 45°F water before becoming completely exhausted, and why?

choices.text: About 10 minutes -- due to loss of muscle and sensory control, a different danger from core-temperature hypothermia
About 10 minutes -- because the core body temperature has already dropped to a dangerous level by that point
choices.misconception: Attributes the exhaustion to core hypothermia, when the manual describes this specific exhaustion as caused by loss of muscle/sensory control, a distinct and earlier danger.

choices.text: Several hours, since swimming generates enough body heat to offset the cold
choices.misconception: Reverses the actual effect -- swimming in cold water accelerates exhaustion and heat loss rather than offsetting the cold.

choices.text: There is no meaningful time limit as long as the swimmer stays calm
choices.misconception: Understates a real, time-limited physical danger; staying calm doesn't remove the muscle/sensory-control risk described.

hints: This is about losing the ABILITY to swim effectively, not about core body temperature dropping.
questions.explanation: A good swimmer in 45°F water becomes completely exhausted after only about 10 minutes of swimming -- due to loss of muscle and sensory control, not core-temperature hypothermia itself, which is a separate, typically slower-developing danger.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Cold Water Survival / Hypothermia section (manual page ~101); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: What is "Cold Incapacitation" in the cold-water danger progression, and roughly when does it occur?

choices.text: Roughly 5 to 15 minutes into cold-water immersion, as decreasing blood flow makes the arms and legs stop responding normally, causing an inability to stay afloat or swim effectively
The first minute of immersion, involving an involuntary gasping reflex
choices.misconception: Describes Cold Shock Response, an earlier and distinct stage, not Cold Incapacitation.

choices.text: A collapse that can occur around the moment of rescue
choices.misconception: Describes Circum-Rescue Collapse, a different, later stage, not Cold Incapacitation.

choices.text: The final, slowest stage of core body temperature loss
choices.misconception: Describes hypothermia itself, the final stage in the progression, not Cold Incapacitation.

hints: This is the second of four stages -- after the first-minute gasp reflex, but before hypothermia itself sets in.
questions.explanation: Cold Incapacitation occurs roughly 5 to 15 minutes into cold-water immersion. Decreasing blood flow makes the arms and legs stop responding normally, causing an inability to stay afloat or swim effectively -- distinct from both the earlier Cold Shock Response and the later stages of Circum-Rescue Collapse and hypothermia.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Cold Water Survival / Hypothermia section, Cold Shock Response 4-stage chart (manual page ~104); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: What is "Circum-Rescue Collapse"?

choices.text: A collapse that can occur before, during, or immediately after rescue, with symptoms ranging from fainting to more serious collapse
Another name for the initial cold-shock gasp reflex
choices.misconception: Confuses Circum-Rescue Collapse with the earlier, distinct Cold Shock Response stage.

choices.text: A collapse that can only occur while the person is still in the water, never after being rescued
choices.misconception: Understates the timing -- Circum-Rescue Collapse can occur before, during, OR after rescue, not only while still in the water.

choices.text: A term for the loss of muscle control that makes swimming difficult
choices.misconception: Describes Cold Incapacitation, a different, earlier stage, not Circum-Rescue Collapse.

hints: This stage is unusual because it isn't tied to a specific time window in the water -- it can happen right around the moment of rescue itself.
questions.explanation: Circum-Rescue Collapse can occur before, during, or immediately after rescue, with symptoms ranging from fainting to more serious collapse -- a reminder that survivors need careful handling right up through and after being pulled from the water, not just while still immersed.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Cold Water Survival / Hypothermia section, Cold Shock Response 4-stage chart (manual page ~104); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: Why might a hypothermia victim appear "drunk or drugged" to someone observing them?

choices.text: Confusion, poor coordination, and personality change are hypothermia symptoms that can resemble intoxication
Cold-water survivors commonly consume alcohol beforehand to cope with the stress
choices.misconception: Invents an unrelated behavioral assumption rather than describing the actual physiological symptom overlap.

choices.text: It's a coincidental resemblance with no real physiological connection
choices.misconception: Understates the connection -- the source explicitly attributes the resemblance to specific overlapping hypothermia symptoms (confusion, poor coordination, personality change), not mere coincidence.

choices.text: Only severe, near-death hypothermia can look this way; mild cases never do
choices.misconception: Overstates the severity threshold -- confusion and coordination problems can appear before the most severe stage.

hints: Think about which cognitive and motor symptoms of hypothermia overlap with common signs of intoxication.
questions.explanation: Confusion, poor coordination, and personality change are all hypothermia symptoms, and together they can make a victim appear "drunk or drugged" even though no alcohol or drugs are involved -- a real physiological overlap, not a coincidence.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Cold Water Survival / Hypothermia section (manual pages ~105-106); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: Why is a rectal temperature reading considered more reliable than an oral reading for a hypothermia victim?

choices.text: The mouth is itself a point of cold-water exposure, making oral readings unreliable for a cold-water victim
Oral thermometers are less accurate than rectal ones in general medical practice
choices.misconception: States a general claim not tied to the actual cold-water-specific reasoning given in the source.

choices.text: Rectal readings are faster to obtain in an emergency
choices.misconception: Speed of measurement isn't the reason given in the source; the stated reason is that the mouth itself has been exposed to cold water, making oral readings unreliable.

choices.text: There is no meaningful difference between the two methods for a hypothermia victim
choices.misconception: Contradicts the source's explicit statement that rectal temperature is the only reliable measurement.

hints: Think about what a cold-water victim's mouth has likely been directly exposed to.
questions.explanation: For a cold-water hypothermia victim, the mouth has itself been a point of cold-water exposure, making an oral temperature reading unreliable. Rectal temperature is called out as the only reliable measurement for this reason.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Cold Water Survival / Hypothermia section (manual page ~105); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: What does this survival training material say about how low a hypothermia victim's core temperature can drop and still allow full recovery?

choices.text: Victims have fully recovered after up to 44 minutes with no apparent life signs and a core temperature as low as 64°F
Recovery is not possible once a victim shows no apparent life signs, regardless of core temperature
choices.misconception: Contradicts the source's explicit persistence point that recovery has occurred even after apparent life signs disappeared.

choices.text: Recovery is only possible if core temperature stays above 90°F at all times
choices.misconception: Understates how low a core temperature the source describes as compatible with full recovery.

choices.text: The source gives no specific figures on this point
choices.misconception: The source does give specific figures on this point (44 minutes, 64 degrees Fahrenheit) -- it isn't silent on the question.

hints: This is meant as an encouraging, 'don't give up early' fact -- a surprisingly low number.
questions.explanation: This is a notable persistence point in the source: victims have fully recovered after up to 44 minutes with no apparent life signs and a core body temperature as low as 64°F (18°C) -- a reminder not to give up on a hypothermia victim too soon.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Cold Water Survival / Hypothermia section (manual page ~107); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: Why should a moderate or severe hypothermia victim be handled as gently as possible, with rescuers doing the moving rather than the victim exerting themselves?

choices.text: The heart becomes unstable at low core temperatures, and exertion or rough handling risks triggering cardiac arrest
Gentle handling is only a comfort measure with no medical basis
choices.misconception: Dismisses a real, stated medical reason (cardiac instability) for the gentle-handling guidance.

choices.text: It's purely to avoid further heat loss through movement, with no cardiac concern involved
choices.misconception: Names a plausible-sounding but incomplete reason -- the source's stated concern is specifically the heart's instability, not just heat loss from movement.

choices.text: It only matters for victims who are already unconscious
choices.misconception: Understates the guidance -- gentle handling matters for any moderate/severe hypothermia victim due to cardiac instability, not only for victims who are already unconscious.

hints: Think about what organ becomes dangerously unstable as core body temperature drops.
questions.explanation: At low core temperatures, the heart becomes unstable -- exertion by the victim or rough handling by a rescuer risks triggering cardiac arrest. This is why rescuers should do the moving, minimize jostling, and avoid letting the victim exert themselves.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Cold Water Survival / Hypothermia section (manual page ~107); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: Which body areas should be prioritized for gentle rewarming on a hypothermia victim, and why?

choices.text: The chest, sides, and groin -- named as the areas of greatest heat loss
The hands and feet, since they are farthest from the body's core
choices.misconception: Names the extremities, which are not the areas the source identifies as priorities for rewarming.

choices.text: The head only, since it accounts for all significant heat loss
choices.misconception: Overstates the head's role in isolation; the source names chest, sides, and groin as the priority areas.

choices.text: There is no particular priority -- rewarm the whole body evenly and quickly
choices.misconception: Contradicts the guidance to rewarm gently and prioritize specific areas, not rush an even, whole-body approach.

hints: These are the same areas the H.E.L.P. position is specifically designed to protect.
questions.explanation: The chest, sides, and groin should be prioritized for gentle rewarming -- these are named as the areas of the body responsible for the greatest heat loss, the same areas the H.E.L.P. position is designed to protect in the first place.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Cold Water Survival / Hypothermia section (manual page ~107); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: What does the H.E.L.P. position involve, and what is its purpose?

choices.text: Crossing your arms close to your chest and crossing/extending your legs downward, to protect the chest, sides, groin, head, and neck from heat loss
Spreading your arms and legs as wide as possible to maximize surface contact with any nearby floating debris
choices.misconception: Describes the opposite body position from H.E.L.P., which draws the limbs in close to protect heat-loss areas, not spread them out.

choices.text: Swimming continuously in a tight circle to generate body heat
choices.misconception: Describes an energy-expending activity, not the H.E.L.P. position, which is a static floating posture.

choices.text: Lying face-down in the water to reduce wind exposure on the back
choices.misconception: Misdescribes the posture -- H.E.L.P. is meant to help a person float face-up, not face-down.

hints: H.E.L.P. stands for Heat Escape Lessening Posture -- think about which body areas you'd want to shield by drawing your limbs in.
questions.explanation: The H.E.L.P. (Heat Escape Lessening Posture) position involves crossing your arms close to your chest and crossing/extending your legs downward. This protects the chest, sides, groin, head, and neck -- the areas of greatest heat loss -- and is claimed to increase survival time by up to 50%.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Cold Water Survival / Hypothermia section (manual page ~103); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: Who takes charge of a survival craft after abandoning ship, in order of priority?

choices.text: The senior ship's officer present, or if none is available, the senior lifeboat-endorsed crew member, or if neither is present, someone the group elects
Whoever boarded the survival craft first
choices.misconception: Boarding order isn't the criterion the manual gives for who takes charge; the stated priority order is based on officer/crew status, not who boarded first.

choices.text: Command is decided randomly to avoid conflict
choices.misconception: Invents an arbitrary selection method not described in the source, which gives a clear priority order.

choices.text: Only a licensed officer may ever take charge; if none is present, no formal leader is designated
choices.misconception: Ignores the source's explicit fallback options (senior lifeboat-endorsed crew member, then an elected leader) when no officer is present.

hints: There's a clear three-tier fallback: officer, then a specific kind of crew member, then an elected choice.
questions.explanation: Command of a survival craft goes to the senior ship's officer present; if none is available, the senior lifeboat-endorsed crew member; if neither is present, someone the group elects as leader.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Organizing for Survival section (manual page ~108); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: Which of the following is NOT one of the survival craft roles the manual describes a leader assigning as part of establishing a routine?

choices.text: Navigator
Ration keeper
choices.misconception: This IS one of the described roles, not the correct 'NOT' answer.

choices.text: Lookouts
choices.misconception: This IS one of the described roles, not the correct 'NOT' answer.

choices.text: Bailers
choices.misconception: This IS one of the described roles, not the correct 'NOT' answer.

hints: The described roles are: ration keeper, lookouts, repair party, bailers, first-aid persons, and someone handling signaling.
questions.explanation: The manual describes assigning a ration keeper, lookouts, a repair party, bailers, first-aid persons, and someone handling signaling -- not a dedicated "navigator" role.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Organizing for Survival section (manual page ~108); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: How is a capsized survival craft righted, and does the method differ between a lifeboat and a liferaft?

choices.text: Yes -- a capsized lifeboat is righted by everyone getting on one side and using grab rails; a raft is righted using righting straps
No -- both a lifeboat and a raft are righted the identical way, using grab rails
choices.misconception: Ignores the described difference -- rafts use righting straps, not grab rails.

choices.text: No -- neither craft type can be righted once capsized; survivors must wait for rescue on the hull
choices.misconception: Contradicts the manual's explicit description of specific recovery methods (grab rails for a lifeboat, righting straps for a raft) -- capsize recovery is described as possible, not something survivors must simply wait out.

choices.text: Yes -- a raft uses grab rails, while a lifeboat uses righting straps
choices.misconception: Reverses which craft type uses which method.

hints: One craft type has rigid structure survivors can grip; the other relies on a dedicated strap system built for the purpose.
questions.explanation: A capsized lifeboat is righted with everyone moving to one side and using grab rails; a raft, which has no rigid structure to grip the same way, is righted using righting straps instead.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Survival on the Sea section (manual page ~109); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: If a survival craft must move in heavy seas, what heading relative to the wind and seas is recommended for steering control while minimizing broaching risk?

choices.text: Wind and seas broad on the bow, at roughly a 30-40 degree angle
Directly stern-to the wind and seas, running with them
choices.misconception: Describes a heading that increases broaching risk rather than minimizing it, the opposite of the recommended guidance.

choices.text: Directly beam-on (broadside) to the wind and seas for maximum stability
choices.misconception: Describes the highest-risk heading for broaching and capsizing, not the recommended one.

choices.text: Heading makes no difference as long as the sea anchor is deployed
choices.misconception: Understates the guidance -- heading relative to the wind and seas matters specifically for steering control and broaching risk, even with a sea anchor or drogue deployed.

hints: This is a headed-mostly-into-the-weather angle, not directly bow-on and not beam-on.
questions.explanation: If movement is necessary in heavy seas, keep the wind and seas broad on the bow, at roughly 30-40 degrees, for steering control while minimizing broaching risk. Preferably, though, stream the drogue and drift bow-into-the-weather rather than trying to make headway at all, unless drifting onto a hazard.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Survival on the Sea section (manual page ~109); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: Why is avoiding seasickness treated as a real survival priority, not just a comfort issue?

choices.text: Vomiting wastes body fluids that are very hard to replace once adrift
Seasickness has no real connection to survival odds, only comfort
choices.misconception: Contradicts the actual reasoning, which ties seasickness directly to fluid loss, a genuine survival concern.

choices.text: It's primarily a concern because it makes noise that could attract predators
choices.misconception: Invents an unrelated reason not described in the source.

choices.text: It matters only for passengers, since crew are assumed to be immune to seasickness
choices.misconception: Invents an unsupported immunity claim; the source describes seasickness as nearly universal in a liferaft, crew included.

hints: Think about what's actually lost from the body when someone vomits, and how hard that is to get back once adrift.
questions.explanation: Seasickness is described as nearly universal in a liferaft and worth actively avoiding because vomiting wastes body fluids that are very hard or impossible to replace once adrift -- a real fluid-preservation concern, not just a comfort issue.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Preserve Body Fluids / Avoid Seasickness section (manual page ~110); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: How soon after boarding a survival craft should you urinate, if you haven't done so shortly before boarding?

choices.text: Within about 2 hours
Within about 15 minutes
choices.misconception: Overstates the urgency -- the actual guidance gives a roughly 2-hour window, not 15 minutes.

choices.text: Only after the first 24 hours have passed
choices.misconception: Confuses this guidance with the separate 24-hour water-rationing rule, an unrelated figure.

choices.text: There is no specific timeframe given
choices.misconception: The manual does give a specific timeframe (roughly 2 hours) rather than leaving this open-ended.

hints: This protects against bladder strain, and trauma from the disaster itself can make it harder to do -- there are even suggested techniques to help.
questions.explanation: You should urinate within about 2 hours of boarding a survival craft if you haven't done so shortly before. Trauma can make this difficult, and the manual offers techniques (like pouring seawater cup-to-cup nearby, or hanging over the side with the lower body in water) that may help.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Urinate Soon After Boarding section (manual page ~110); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: According to this survival training material, roughly how long can the body survive on as little as 11 ounces of water per day with no food?

choices.text: Over 40 days
About 10 days
choices.misconception: Describes the survival window associated with a smaller ration (2-3 ounces per day), not the 11-ounce figure.

choices.text: About 4 days
choices.misconception: Describes the onset of delirium with NO water at all, not the 11-ounce-per-day figure.

choices.text: Only a few hours
choices.misconception: Drastically understates the figure -- the source describes a much longer survival window (over 40 days) on this water ration, not a matter of hours.

hints: This is the most generous of the water-ration survival figures given -- and it's a surprisingly long time.
questions.explanation: The body can survive over 40 days on as little as 11 ounces of water per day with no food. A smaller ration of 2-3 ounces per day can still maintain health for up to 10 days, while with no water at all, delirium sets in around 4 days and death follows in about 8-12 days.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Drinking Water section (manual page ~112); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: After the first 24 hours, what is the full daily water ration per person, and how should it be divided?

choices.text: One pint per day, split into three equal doses at sunrise, midday, and sunset
Three liters per day, consumed all at once each morning
choices.misconception: Confuses the daily rationing rate (1 pint per day once adrift) with the separate 3-liter figure, which is the total amount of water required to be stowed aboard a lifeboat -- a stowage requirement, not a daily ration.

choices.text: Whatever amount each survivor personally requests, with no fixed schedule
choices.misconception: Contradicts the actual guidance, which sets a fixed daily amount and a fixed three-dose schedule.

choices.text: One pint per day, consumed in a single dose whenever convenient
choices.misconception: Gets the daily amount right but misses the specific three-dose schedule (sunrise, midday, sunset).

hints: This is a RATE of use once adrift, not the total amount required to be stowed before anyone even boards.
questions.explanation: After the first 24 hours, the full daily ration is one pint of water per person, divided into three equal doses at sunrise, midday, and sunset. This is a daily rationing rate, a different fact from the separate requirement for how much water must be stowed aboard a lifeboat in total.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Drinking Water section (manual page ~112); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: How can you tell safe, old sea ice apart from unsafe "salt ice"?

choices.text: Safe old sea ice is bluish with smooth, rounded corners; unsafe salt ice is gray and milky
Safe sea ice is white and opaque; unsafe salt ice is clear and glassy
choices.misconception: Reverses the actual visual cues described -- safe ice is bluish, unsafe salt ice is the gray/milky one.

choices.text: There is no visual way to tell them apart; taste is the only reliable test
choices.misconception: Ignores the visual cues (color, corner shape) the source describes as distinguishing the two.

choices.text: Both types are equally safe to eat or drink once melted
choices.misconception: Contradicts the manual's explicit warning that salt ice should not be eaten, regardless of melting.

hints: One type has had time to lose its salt content and shows it visually; the other is freshly formed and still salty.
questions.explanation: Old, weathered sea ice is bluish in color with smooth, rounded corners, and is usually pure enough to eat or drink. Freshly formed "salt ice" is gray and milky and should not be eaten.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Snow / Ice section (manual page ~113); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: Why should you never drink seawater to stretch your fresh water supply?

choices.text: It worsens thirst, accelerates fluid loss through the kidneys and intestines, and can cause delirium, convulsions, and death
It's technically safe but simply tastes unpleasant, discouraging most survivors from trying it
choices.misconception: Understates the danger -- the source describes serious physiological harm (worsened thirst, fluid loss, delirium, convulsions, death), not just an unpleasant taste.

choices.text: It's fine in small amounts mixed with fresh water, but dangerous if consumed alone
choices.misconception: Contradicts the source's explicit warning against mixing salt water with fresh water at all, not just drinking it alone.

choices.text: It only causes mild, temporary stomach upset
choices.misconception: Understates the danger described -- the source's warning covers serious, potentially fatal effects, not just mild stomach upset.

hints: This is a serious physiological warning, not just a matter of taste.
questions.explanation: Drinking seawater worsens thirst, accelerates fluid loss through the kidneys and intestines, and can cause delirium, convulsions, and death -- never mix or substitute salt water (or urine or animal fluids) for fresh water, even to try to stretch a limited supply.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Snow / Ice, and the seawater/urine warning section (manual page ~113); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: Why is it dangerous to eat food without available drinking water?

choices.text: The body needs water to digest food, and eating without it can be fatal
It isn't actually dangerous -- food should always be eaten as soon as it's available regardless of water supply
choices.misconception: Contradicts the source's explicit warning against eating without available water.

choices.text: Food without water simply tastes unpleasant, with no real safety concern
choices.misconception: Understates the guidance -- the source describes the water-for-digestion need as general, not limited to high-protein foods specifically.

choices.text: It's only a concern for high-protein foods, not the carbohydrate-heavy emergency provisions
choices.misconception: Understates the general rule -- the source describes needing water to digest food generally, not just protein specifically.

hints: Digestion itself requires a resource you might be trying to conserve.
questions.explanation: The body needs water to digest food, so eating without available water can be fatal. This is part of why survival-craft rations are deliberately low-protein and salt-free -- both protein and salt cost extra body fluid to process, on top of digestion's baseline water need.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Emergency Provisions / Food section (manual pages ~113); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: What kind of cloud formation can be a sign that land is nearby?

choices.text: Dense, white, flat-based, fluffy cumulus clouds forming over a specific area, rather than spread broadly across the sky
Thin, wispy, high-altitude clouds spread evenly across the whole sky
choices.misconception: Describes a different cloud type not associated with the land-proximity sign given in the source.

choices.text: Dark storm clouds concentrated directly overhead
choices.misconception: Confuses a storm indicator with the specific fair-weather cumulus formation the source describes as a land sign.

choices.text: Any cloud cover is an equally reliable sign of nearby land
choices.misconception: Overstates the reliability of cloud cover in general; the source specifically ties this particular cumulus formation, not clouds generally, to nearby land.

hints: Think about why land, unlike open water, would cause a cloud to form specifically above it rather than everywhere equally.
questions.explanation: Dense, white, flat-based, fluffy cumulus clouds forming over a specific area -- rather than covering the sky broadly -- can indicate nearby land, since rising warm air over land (versus the more stable air temperature over water) creates them.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Looking for Land section (manual pages ~115-117); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: Why does the manual recommend avoiding a surf landing unless necessary?

choices.text: Professional rescue crews train and equip specifically for surf landings, conditions that ordinary merchant-vessel survival craft and crews usually lack -- especially with children or elderly survivors aboard
Surf landings are prohibited by international regulation in all circumstances
choices.misconception: Invents a regulatory prohibition; the actual guidance is practical risk-based advice, not a blanket rule.

choices.text: Surf landings always destroy the survival craft regardless of technique
choices.misconception: Overstates the danger as an absolute outcome; the guidance is about elevated risk and lacking specialized training/equipment, not a certainty of destruction regardless of technique.

choices.text: There is no particular danger; the caution is purely a matter of tradition
choices.misconception: Dismisses a real, practical safety reason (lack of specialized training and equipment) as if it were merely traditional caution.

hints: Compare who normally handles this kind of landing professionally to who's actually doing it in a survival situation.
questions.explanation: Professional rescue crews train and equip specifically for surf landings -- conditions and skills that ordinary merchant-vessel survival craft and crews usually lack, especially with children or elderly survivors aboard. That's why a surf landing should be avoided unless it's genuinely necessary.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Beaching Survival Craft section (manual page ~116); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: How many portable VHF survival craft transceivers are required aboard a passenger ship or a cargo vessel of 500 gross tons or over?

choices.text: Three
Two
choices.misconception: Two is the number required on smaller cargo vessels (300-500 gross tons), not on passenger ships or vessels 500 gross tons and over.

choices.text: One
choices.misconception: Understates the requirement -- one radio is not the required minimum on any of the vessel classes described.

choices.text: Five
choices.misconception: Overstates the requirement -- five radios is not a figure given anywhere in the source.

hints: There's a different, smaller number required on smaller cargo vessels between 300 and 500 tons.
questions.explanation: Three portable handheld VHF survival craft transceivers are required aboard passenger ships and cargo vessels 500 gross tons and over. Smaller cargo vessels between 300 and 500 tons only require two.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, VHF Survival Craft Transceiver / SCT section (manual page ~120); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: Which VHF channel should be used first in an emergency, and which channel is used for search-and-rescue communications with the Coast Guard and aircraft?

choices.text: Channel 16 first for the emergency itself; channel 6 for SAR communications
Channel 6 first for the emergency itself; channel 16 for SAR communications
choices.misconception: Reverses the two channels' roles.

choices.text: Both channels serve the same purpose interchangeably
choices.misconception: Ignores the distinct, separate role each channel plays.

choices.text: Channel 16 for both purposes; channel 6 is unused in survival situations
choices.misconception: Ignores channel 6's specific SAR-communications role.

hints: One channel is specifically for the initial emergency call; the other is for ongoing coordination with rescuers.
questions.explanation: Channel 16 (156.8 MHz) should be used first in an emergency. Channel 6 is used for search-and-rescue communications with the Coast Guard and aircraft.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, VHF Survival Craft Transceiver / SCT section (manual page ~120); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: What are the watertightness and battery specifications for a VHF survival craft transceiver?

choices.text: Watertight to 1 meter for 5 minutes, with a battery capacity of 8 hours of operation
Watertight to 10 meters for 5 minutes, with a battery capacity of 48 hours of operation
choices.misconception: These are the SART's and EPIRB's own separate specifications (10m/5min watertight for SART; 48-hour minimum transmit time for EPIRB), not the VHF transceiver's.

choices.text: Not water-resistant at all, with a battery capacity of 8 hours of operation
choices.misconception: Understates the radio's watertightness -- it is rated watertight to 1 meter for 5 minutes.

choices.text: Watertight to 1 meter for 5 minutes, with an unlimited battery capacity
choices.misconception: Overstates the battery capacity, which is specifically 8 hours, not unlimited.

hints: Don't mix this radio's specs up with the EPIRB's or SART's own separate watertight/duration ratings.
questions.explanation: A VHF survival craft transceiver is watertight to 1 meter for 5 minutes, and its battery has a capacity of 8 hours of operation -- different figures from the EPIRB's 48-hour minimum transmit time or the SART's 10-meter/5-minute watertight rating.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, VHF Survival Craft Transceiver / SCT section (manual page ~120); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: When should a survival craft transceiver be turned on?

choices.text: Only when you know there is a vessel or rescue aircraft nearby
Continuously, from the moment you board the survival craft
choices.misconception: Wastes limited battery capacity by running continuously rather than only when a rescuer is known to be nearby.

choices.text: Only after the first 24 hours have passed
choices.misconception: Confuses this guidance with the unrelated 24-hour food/water rationing rule.

choices.text: Only at night, to conserve the battery during daylight
choices.misconception: Invents a day/night restriction that isn't the actual guidance, which is about proximity to a known rescuer, not time of day.

hints: Think about the radio's limited battery life, and why running it constantly on the chance someone might be listening would be wasteful.
questions.explanation: The radio should not be turned on unless you know there is a vessel or rescue aircraft nearby -- the radios are designed for unskilled operators who are in sight of a rescue craft, and battery life is limited.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, VHF Survival Craft Transceiver / SCT section (manual page ~120); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: If a survival craft transceiver uses rechargeable Ni-Cad batteries, what should be done before recharging?

choices.text: The battery should be allowed to run down before recharging
The battery should be recharged as frequently as possible, regardless of remaining charge
choices.misconception: Reverses the actual guidance -- Ni-Cad batteries should be run down before recharging, not topped off frequently.

choices.text: The radio must be sent to an approved facility for any battery service
choices.misconception: Invents a facility requirement not described for this specific radio; the guidance given is a simple usage habit, not a service restriction.

choices.text: Ni-Cad batteries in this radio cannot be recharged at all
choices.misconception: Contradicts the source, which explicitly describes Ni-Cad batteries as usable and rechargeable in this radio.

hints: This is a habit related to how Ni-Cad battery chemistry behaves over repeated partial charges.
questions.explanation: If the survival craft transceiver uses rechargeable Ni-Cad batteries, the battery should be allowed to run down before recharging.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, VHF Survival Craft Transceiver / SCT section (manual page ~120); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: Where is a SOLAS-required Class I EPIRB normally located, and why?

choices.text: On the flying bridge or bridge wing, so its stowed location allows for unobstructed automatic float-free operation
Inside a locked cabin, to prevent accidental activation
choices.misconception: Would defeat the EPIRB's automatic float-free release function, which requires an unobstructed path if the ship sinks.

choices.text: In the engine room, for easy access by the engineering crew
choices.misconception: Places the EPIRB somewhere that would prevent automatic float-free release if the ship sank, and is not the described stowage location.

choices.text: Location doesn't matter as long as it's registered with NOAA
choices.misconception: Registration matters for a different reason (feeding the SAR database), but doesn't address the stowage-location requirement this question is asking about.

hints: A Class I EPIRB is specifically capable of float-free operation -- think about what stowage location that function requires.
questions.explanation: A SOLAS-required ship carries at least 1 Class I EPIRB (capable of float-free operation), normally located on the flying bridge or bridge wing, so its stowed location allows for unobstructed automatic float-free release if the vessel sinks.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, Emergency Position Indicating Radio Beacon (EPIRB) section (manual page ~121); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: What must a US-flagged vessel's EPIRB be registered with, and what database does that feed?

choices.text: NOAA (National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration), feeding the 406 MHz Registration Database used by SAR organizations worldwide
The Coast Guard's local district office only, with no wider database
choices.misconception: Understates the scope of the registration -- it feeds a database available to SAR organizations worldwide, not just a local office.

choices.text: The vessel's insurance provider
choices.misconception: Invents an insurance-related registration requirement not described in the source.

choices.text: No registration is required for EPIRBs on US-flagged vessels
choices.misconception: Contradicts the explicit NOAA registration requirement for US-flagged vessels.

hints: This registration feeds a specific frequency-named database used internationally by search-and-rescue organizations.
questions.explanation: EPIRBs must be registered with NOAA for US-flagged vessels. That registration information feeds the 406 MHz Registration Database, available to SAR organizations worldwide, including the vessel's name and number of passengers.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, EPIRB section (manual page ~121); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: What satellite system does an EPIRB rely on, and roughly how long does it currently take on average to detect a distress beacon?

choices.text: The COSPAS-SARSAT system, averaging around 45 minutes for distress beacon detection
The COSPAS-SARSAT system, averaging around 5 minutes for distress beacon detection
choices.misconception: Understates the average detection time considerably.

choices.text: The COSPAS-SARSAT system, averaging around 24 hours for distress beacon detection
choices.misconception: Greatly overstates the average detection time.

choices.text: GPS alone, with no dedicated search-and-rescue satellite network involved
choices.misconception: Understates the system -- EPIRB detection relies on a dedicated SAR satellite network (COSPAS-SARSAT) combined with cooperating satellites, not GPS alone.

hints: This program has been running since the 1980s, combining dedicated search-and-rescue satellites with cooperating satellite networks.
questions.explanation: An EPIRB relies on the COSPAS-SARSAT system, in place since the 1980s, which combines dedicated search-and-rescue satellites with cooperating satellite networks for worldwide beacon detection. In its current configuration, the program averages around 45 minutes for distress beacon detection, with a much lower time over most of the world's ship-transited ocean surface.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, EPIRB section (manual page ~121); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: An EPIRB broadcasts on two different frequencies for two different purposes. What are they?

choices.text: 406 MHz at 5 watts to satellites for initial detection, and a lower-power 121.5 MHz homing signal for nearby SAR assets to zero in on the exact position
156.8 MHz for the initial satellite alert, and 406 MHz as the homing signal
choices.misconception: Confuses the EPIRB's own frequencies with VHF channel 16's frequency (156.8 MHz), and reverses which frequency serves which role.

choices.text: A single frequency is used for both satellite detection and close-range homing
choices.misconception: Understates the EPIRB's design, which specifically uses two separate frequencies for two separate purposes.

choices.text: 9 GHz to satellites, and 406 MHz as the homing signal
choices.misconception: 9 GHz is the SART's radar-detection frequency, not one of the EPIRB's own broadcast frequencies.

hints: One frequency reaches satellites far overhead; the other is a shorter-range signal meant for rescuers who are already close by.
questions.explanation: An EPIRB broadcasts its main distress signal to satellites at 406 MHz / 5 watts, and separately broadcasts a lower-power 121.5 MHz homing signal specifically for nearby SAR assets to zero in on the exact position -- two frequencies for two different jobs.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, EPIRB section (manual page ~121); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: For how long must an EPIRB be able to transmit once activated?

choices.text: At least 48 hours
At least 8 hours
choices.misconception: 8 hours is the VHF survival craft transceiver's own separate battery specification, not the EPIRB's minimum transmit duration.

choices.text: At least 96 hours
choices.misconception: 96 hours is the SART's standby-mode battery requirement, not the EPIRB's minimum transmit duration.

choices.text: There is no minimum transmit duration specified
choices.misconception: Contradicts the source, which gives an explicit minimum transmit duration.

hints: This is the same figure as the minimum wait time before deploying a second EPIRB, if more than one is available.
questions.explanation: An EPIRB must be able to transmit for at least 48 hours once activated -- the same figure behind the guidance that if more than one EPIRB is available, only one should be deployed at a time, waiting 48 hours before deploying the second.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, EPIRB section (manual page ~121); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: Once a survival craft is clear of a sinking vessel, how should a manually-deployed EPIRB be handled?

choices.text: Place it in the water secured to the survival craft by its lanyard, and don't grab the antenna or remove it from the water once it's broadcasting
Keep it inside the survival craft at all times to protect it from waves
choices.misconception: Keeping the EPIRB inside the craft, rather than in the water on its lanyard, works against the water-grounding effect that produces a strong signal, and can obstruct its antenna's view of the sky.

choices.text: Hold the antenna upright by hand to maximize signal strength
choices.misconception: Handling the antenna is explicitly discouraged -- it interferes with signal quality rather than improving it.

choices.text: Turn it off periodically to conserve battery life once activated
choices.misconception: Contradicts the explicit guidance that once activated, an EPIRB should never be turned off -- it must broadcast continuously to work correctly.

hints: The water itself plays a role in producing a strong signal -- think about why the EPIRB should stay in the water rather than being brought aboard.
questions.explanation: Once the survival craft is clear of the sinking vessel, the EPIRB should be placed in the water and secured to the craft with its lanyard -- the water grounds the battery and produces a strong signal. Don't grab the antenna or remove it from the water, since this interferes with signal quality, and once activated, never turn the EPIRB off.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, EPIRB section (manual page ~122); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: What kind of radar is required to detect a SART's signal?

choices.text: X-Band, 9 GHz (3 cm) navigational radar
Any radar band will detect a SART equally well
choices.misconception: Understates the requirement -- a SART is specifically designed to be detected by X-Band, 9 GHz navigational radar, not any radar band.

choices.text: S-Band, 3 GHz weather radar
choices.misconception: Names the wrong radar band and frequency; the SART's design frequency is 9 GHz X-Band.

choices.text: A SART is detected by satellite, not by shipboard radar
choices.misconception: Confuses SART detection (shipboard X-Band radar) with EPIRB detection (satellite-based), two different devices with two different detection methods.

hints: This is a specific radar band and frequency, not detection by satellite.
questions.explanation: A SART's signal is picked up on a ship's X-Band, 9 GHz (3 cm) navigational radar -- unlike an EPIRB, which is detected via satellite, not shipboard radar.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, Search and Rescue Radar Transponder (SART) section (manual page ~123); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: How does a SART's signal appear on a rescuing ship's radar screen?

choices.text: As a series of 12 blips or dots extending outward from the SART's position, which turn into arcs as the rescue craft closes in
As a single, continuously blinking dot with no change as range decreases
choices.misconception: Understates the SART's distinctive radar signature, which specifically changes shape (blips turning into arcs) as range decreases.

choices.text: As a solid, unbroken circle around the SART's position at all ranges
choices.misconception: Understates the SART's distinctive signature -- a solid unbroken circle isn't the described appearance; the actual pattern is blips turning into arcs as range decreases.

choices.text: As a text label displaying the vessel's name directly on the radar screen
choices.misconception: Radar cannot display text; the SART is identified by its distinctive blip-to-arc radar signature, not a text label.

hints: The signal's appearance actually changes shape as the rescuing vessel gets closer -- that's part of what makes it recognizable.
questions.explanation: A SART's signal appears on radar as a series of 12 blips or dots extending outward from its position, which turn into arcs as the rescue craft closes the distance -- a distinctive pattern designed to be immediately recognizable to a radar operator.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, SART section (manual page ~123); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: How should a SART be rigged and activated once deployed?

choices.text: Rigged about 1 meter above the water in standby mode; it automatically activates, with an audio and/or visual indication, when struck by a nearby radar pulse
It launches and activates fully automatically, with no rigging or standby mode involved
choices.misconception: Contradicts the source's explicit statement that SARTs are not designed to launch and operate automatically -- they must be rigged and set to standby mode by a person.

choices.text: Submerged fully underwater, activating on contact with seawater
choices.misconception: Misdescribes both the rigging height (about 1 meter above the water, not submerged) and the activation trigger (a nearby radar pulse, not water contact).

choices.text: It must remain in transponder mode at all times, with no standby mode available
choices.misconception: Ignores the standby mode the SART is set to before activation -- it doesn't stay in transponder mode continuously from the start.

hints: A SART needs a person to rig and arm it -- it doesn't do this step on its own.
questions.explanation: A SART is rigged about 1 meter above the water and turned on to standby mode. It automatically activates -- usually with an audio and/or visual indication -- when a nearby radar pulse strikes it, and also has a manual activate/deactivate switch. SARTs are not designed to launch and operate automatically; a person must rig and arm one first.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, SART section (manual page ~123); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: For how many hours must a SART's battery operate in standby mode?

choices.text: 96 hours
48 hours
choices.misconception: 48 hours is the EPIRB's minimum transmit duration, not the SART's standby-mode battery requirement.

choices.text: 8 hours
choices.misconception: 8 hours is the VHF survival craft transceiver's battery capacity, not the SART's standby-mode requirement.

choices.text: There is no minimum standby-mode battery requirement specified
choices.misconception: Contradicts the source, which gives an explicit standby-mode battery requirement.

hints: This is the longest of the three signaling devices' battery-duration figures covered in this chapter.
questions.explanation: A SART's battery must operate for 96 hours in standby mode, the longest standby/duration figure among the VHF transceiver (8 hours), EPIRB (48-hour minimum transmit), and SART covered in this chapter.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, SART section (manual page ~123); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md. Note: the source also specifies an additional transponder-mode battery duration beyond the 96-hour standby figure, but that specific number did not survive OCR in this batch and is not used here -- see the source intake's OCR-uncertainty notes.

questions.prompt: What is the IMO performance standard for a SART's minimum detection range, and how far should a Coast Guard aircraft flying at 3,000 feet be able to detect one?
