choices.text: At least 5 miles when positioned 1 meter above sea level; an aircraft at 3,000 feet should detect it at 30 miles
At least 30 miles at sea level in all conditions, with no altitude-dependent figure given
choices.misconception: Reverses the two figures -- 5 miles is the sea-level standard, and 30 miles is specifically the aircraft-at-altitude detection distance, not a universal sea-level figure.

choices.text: At least 1 mile when positioned 1 meter above sea level, with no aircraft-specific figure given
choices.misconception: Understates the required range considerably -- the actual IMO standard is 5 miles at 1 meter height, not 1 mile.

choices.text: Range is unaffected by the SART's height above the water
choices.misconception: Contradicts the source, which explicitly ties detection range to height above the water.

hints: Detection range increases with height above the water -- an aircraft at altitude can detect a SART from much farther away than a nearby ship at sea level.
questions.explanation: The IMO performance standard requires a SART to have a detection range of at least 5 miles when positioned at least 1 meter above sea level -- range increases with height, so a Coast Guard aircraft flying a search pattern at 3,000 feet should detect the SART at a distance of 30 miles.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, SART section (manual page ~123); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: What durability standards must a SART meet, and how many are required on a passenger vessel or a vessel over 500 tons?

choices.text: Survive a 20-meter drop into the water without damage and remain watertight to 10 meters for 5 minutes; two SARTs are required, stowed one on each side of the vessel
Survive a 20-meter drop and remain watertight to 10 meters for 5 minutes; only one SART is required regardless of vessel size
choices.misconception: Gets the durability figures right but misstates the quantity requirement -- passenger vessels and vessels over 500 tons require two SARTs, not one.

choices.text: Survive a 3-meter drop and remain watertight to 1 meter for 5 minutes; two SARTs are required
choices.misconception: Understates the durability figures -- the actual standards are a 20-meter drop and 10-meter/5-minute watertight rating, considerably higher than stated here.

choices.text: No specific drop or watertight standard is specified; only a quantity requirement exists
choices.misconception: Contradicts the source, which gives explicit drop-height and watertight figures alongside the quantity requirement.

hints: Two separate durability figures (a drop height and a watertight depth/duration) plus a quantity-and-placement rule for larger vessels.
questions.explanation: A SART is designed to survive a 20-meter drop into the water without damage, and remain watertight to 10 meters for 5 minutes. On passenger vessels and vessels over 500 tons, two SARTs are required, one stowed on each side of the vessel (vessels between 300-500 tons require only one).
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, SART section (manual page ~123); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: What signal indicates "this is the best place to land" from a shore party or another craft?

choices.text: Vertical motion of a white flag or arms (by day), or vertical motion of a white flag/flare, a green star signal, or code letter K by light/sound (day or night)
Horizontal motion of a white flag or arms, or a red star signal
choices.misconception: Describes the 'landing here is highly dangerous' signal, the opposite meaning, not 'best place to land.'

choices.text: Any color smoke signal, day or night
choices.misconception: Not part of the described 'best place to land' signal set -- smoke signal color isn't used to convey this particular message in the source.

choices.text: Three white star rockets fired one minute apart
choices.misconception: Describes the 'you are seen, help will be given as soon as possible' reply signal, a different message from 'best place to land.'

hints: Vertical motion, a green star, or the letter K -- contrast this with the horizontal-motion, red-star signal for the opposite message.
questions.explanation: "This is the best place to land" is signaled by vertical motion of a white flag or arms (by day), or by vertical motion of a white flag/flare, firing a green star signal, or signaling code letter K (dash-dot-dash) by light or sound (day or night).
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, Signals to Communicate From Shore section (manual page ~125); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: What signal indicates "landing here is highly dangerous"?

choices.text: Horizontal motion of arms or a white flag/flare (by day), or horizontal motion of a white flag/light/flare, a red star signal, or code letter S by light/sound (day or night)
Vertical motion of arms or a white flag, or a green star signal
choices.misconception: Describes the 'best place to land' signal, the opposite meaning, not 'landing here is highly dangerous.'

choices.text: A steady white light placed at a lower level
choices.misconception: Describes a reference technique used to help a landing party approach in a direct line, not the specific 'landing here is highly dangerous' signal itself.

choices.text: Firing a rocket parachute flare with a red light
choices.misconception: Describes a general distress signal for attracting attention from a distance, not the specific shore-to-craft 'landing is dangerous' signal.

hints: Horizontal motion, a red star, or the letter S -- the opposite motion and color from the 'best place to land' signal.
questions.explanation: "Landing here is highly dangerous" is signaled by holding arms horizontally or horizontal motion of a white flag/flare (by day), or by horizontal motion of a white flag/light/flare, firing a red star signal, or signaling code letter S (dot-dot-dot) by sound or light (day or night).
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, Signals to Communicate From Shore section (manual page ~126); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: If a shore party signals code letter S followed by code letter R, what are they communicating?

choices.text: Landing here is dangerous, but a better landing place is located more to the right, as seen by the survival craft's occupants facing the shore
Landing here is dangerous, but a better landing place is located more to the left
choices.misconception: Reverses the direction -- code letter R specifically indicates the better landing place is to the right, not the left (that's code letter L).

choices.text: This is the best place to land, with no direction change needed
choices.misconception: Misreads the code letters -- S followed by R or L specifically signals a direction for a better landing place, not that the current location is the best place to land.

choices.text: "Right" and "left" here refer to the shore party's own orientation, not the survival craft's
choices.misconception: Reverses whose perspective 'right' and 'left' are defined from -- the manual specifies these directions are as seen by the survival craft's occupants facing the shore, not the shore party's own orientation.

hints: R and L are used the way you'd expect (Right, Left) -- but whose 'right' and 'left' are they describing?
questions.explanation: Code letter S followed by R means landing here is dangerous, but a better landing place is located more to the right; S followed by L means more to the left. "Right" and "left" are specifically defined as seen by the survival craft's occupants facing the shore -- not the shore party's own perspective.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, Signals to Communicate From Shore section (manual page ~126); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: How does an aircraft signal that it is directing a surface craft toward a vessel in distress, versus dismissing it from further assistance?

choices.text: Directing: circling the surface craft and crossing its projected course close ahead at low altitude (with throttle or propeller-pitch changes), then heading in the direction to go. Dismissing: crossing the surface craft's wake close astern at low altitude, with similar throttle/propeller-pitch changes
Directing: crossing the wake close astern. Dismissing: crossing the course close ahead
choices.misconception: Reverses the two maneuvers' meanings.

choices.text: Both signals use the identical maneuver, distinguished only by the aircraft's altitude
choices.misconception: Contradicts the source, which describes two distinct maneuvers (crossing ahead vs. crossing astern), not one identical maneuver distinguished only by altitude.

choices.text: An aircraft has no standardized way to dismiss a surface craft from assistance -- only to direct it
choices.misconception: Ignores the specific, standardized dismissal signal (crossing the wake close astern at low altitude).

hints: One maneuver crosses in FRONT of the surface craft's path (ahead); the other crosses BEHIND it (astern) -- think about which direction implies 'follow me' versus 'you're no longer needed.'
questions.explanation: An aircraft directing a surface craft toward a vessel in distress circles the surface craft, crosses its projected course close ahead at low altitude while opening/closing the throttle or changing propeller pitch, then heads in the direction the surface craft should go. To dismiss the surface craft from further assistance, the aircraft instead crosses its wake close astern at low altitude, with the same throttle/propeller-pitch changes.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, Aircraft-to-surface-craft signals section (manual page ~127); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: Besides firing a gun once per minute, what other sound-based signal is on the international list of recognized distress signals?

choices.text: Constant sounding of any fog signal apparatus
A single short blast of a ship's whistle
choices.misconception: A single short blast is not one of the 16 items on the international distress-signal list described in the source.

choices.text: Three short blasts followed by three long blasts
choices.misconception: This specific blast pattern is not one of the 16 items on the international distress-signal list described in the source.

choices.text: Sounding the fog signal apparatus only once, briefly
choices.misconception: Understates the signal -- the recognized distress signal is constant, continuous sounding, not a single brief sound.

hints: This is a continuous, ongoing sound, distinct from the once-a-minute gun signal.
questions.explanation: Constant sounding of any fog signal apparatus is a separate recognized distress signal from firing a gun or other explosive once per minute -- both are sound-based, but one is continuous and the other is a repeated single blast.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, International Distress Signals section (manual page ~130); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: What visual distress signal involves burning something like a tar barrel or oil barrel aboard the vessel?

choices.text: Creating flames on the vessel
Igniting an orange smoke signal
choices.misconception: Orange smoke is a separate, specific pyrotechnic distress signal, not the same as creating open flames on the vessel itself.

choices.text: Firing a rocket parachute flare
choices.misconception: Firing a rocket parachute flare is a separate, distinct item on the distress-signal list, not the same as creating open flames aboard the vessel.

choices.text: Setting a dye marker
choices.misconception: Setting a dye marker is a separate, distinct item on the distress-signal list, not the same as creating open flames aboard the vessel.

hints: This is a recognized distress signal that predates modern pyrotechnics -- literally setting something on fire aboard the vessel.
questions.explanation: Creating flames on the vessel -- for example, burning a tar barrel or oil barrel -- is one of the 16 recognized international distress signals, distinct from more modern pyrotechnic signals like orange smoke or parachute flares.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, International Distress Signals section (manual page ~130); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: What distress signal is specifically meant to be identified from the air?

choices.text: Flying a piece of orange-colored canvas with a black square and circle (or other identifying symbol)
The N.C. international code flag hoist
choices.misconception: The N.C. flag hoist is meant to be read by other vessels at or near sea level, not specifically designed as an aerial-identification signal.

choices.text: A steady white light placed at a lower level
choices.misconception: This is the reference-light technique used to help a landing party approach in a direct line, not the signal described as being for identification from the air.

choices.text: Standing on deck with arms outstretched, slowly raising and lowering them
choices.misconception: This is a close-range, person-to-person visual signal, not the specific signal described as being for identification from the air.

hints: Think about what would be easiest to spot and recognize from above, looking down at a vessel.
questions.explanation: Flying a piece of orange-colored canvas marked with a black square and circle (or other appropriate identifying symbol) is a distress signal specifically meant for identification from the air.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, International Distress Signals section (manual page ~130); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: The N.C. distress signal is a hoist of two international code flags. What does each flag look like?

choices.text: November is a white-and-blue checkered flag; Charlie is horizontal stripes of blue, white, red, white, blue
November is solid orange; Charlie is solid black
choices.misconception: Invents flag designs not described in the source, which specifies a checkered pattern for November and horizontal stripes for Charlie.

choices.text: Both flags are identical, plain white flags
choices.misconception: Contradicts the source's specific description of a checkered pattern for November and horizontal stripes for Charlie -- neither flag is described as plain white.

choices.text: The specific flag designs are not standardized and vary by vessel
choices.misconception: Contradicts the source's specific, standardized description of each flag's design.

hints: One flag has a checkered pattern in two colors; the other has five horizontal stripes.
questions.explanation: The N.C. distress hoist combines two international code flags: November, a white-and-blue checkered flag, and Charlie, which has horizontal stripes of blue, white, red, white, and blue.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, International Distress Signals section (manual page ~130); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: The international distress-signal list includes several radio/electronic-based items beyond speaking the word "Mayday." Which of the following is one of them?

choices.text: Sending the radiotelephone alarm signal
Broadcasting on VHF channel 6 exclusively
choices.misconception: Channel 6 is used for ongoing SAR communications, not listed as its own distinct international distress signal the way the radiotelephone alarm signal is.

choices.text: Repeating the ship's name three times
choices.misconception: Invents a signal not described in the source, which lists a specific, named radiotelephone alarm signal instead.

choices.text: There is no other radio-based signal on the list besides speaking "Mayday"
choices.misconception: Understates the list -- the radiotelephone alarm signal is a separate, additional radio-based item on the same list.

hints: This is a distinct signal from the spoken word "Mayday" itself -- a specific alarm signal sent over radiotelephone.
questions.explanation: Sending the radiotelephone alarm signal is a separate item on the international distress-signal list, distinct from speaking the word "Mayday." The list also separately includes transmitting SOS via an EPIRB and a ship-to-shore distress alert via Inmarsat or another mobile satellite device -- three different electronic/radio-based signals beyond the spoken Mayday call.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, International Distress Signals section (manual page ~130); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: How should pyrotechnic signals be fired from a survival craft?

choices.text: Downwind, on the leeward side of the craft, at an angle of about 60 degrees above the horizon, held at a slight angle over the water
Upwind, on the windward side of the craft, straight up
choices.misconception: Reverses both the direction (leeward, not windward) and the angle guidance given in the source for firing pyrotechnics safely.

choices.text: Directly overhead at a 90-degree angle, from any side of the craft
choices.misconception: Both the direction (any side, rather than specifically leeward) and the angle (90 degrees straight up, rather than about 60 degrees) are wrong.

choices.text: Direction and angle don't matter as long as the signal is visible
choices.misconception: Ignores the specific safety reasoning behind firing downwind and at an angle over the water -- burning particles fall and can injure survivors or damage the raft.

hints: Burning particles fall from these signals -- think about which side and angle keeps them away from the survivors and the craft.
questions.explanation: Pyrotechnic signals should be fired downwind on the leeward side of the survival craft, at an angle of about 60 degrees above the horizon, held at a slight angle over the water -- since burning particles fall and could burn survivors or damage the liferaft otherwise.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, Signaling Devices section (manual page ~131); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: How should a signaling mirror be used most effectively while the sun is out?

choices.text: Flash it all around the entire horizon continuously, rather than waiting until a rescue craft is actually visible
Only aim it directly at a rescue craft once one is spotted, and never before
choices.misconception: Wastes the mirror's advantage -- an aircraft can spot the flash from farther away than you would see the aircraft, so flashing around the whole horizon continuously is more effective than waiting.

choices.text: Only use it at night, since sunlight would wash out the flash
choices.misconception: Reverses the actual requirement -- a signaling mirror specifically requires sunlight to work, making it a daytime-only device.

choices.text: Save it for a single, one-time use, since it can only be used once
choices.misconception: Understates the mirror's reusability -- it can be used repeatedly, unlike a pyrotechnic flare.

hints: The aircraft that spots your flash may be farther away than you can currently see -- what does that suggest about when to start signaling?
questions.explanation: A signaling mirror should be flashed all around the horizon continuously whenever the sun is shining, rather than waiting to spot a rescue craft first -- an aircraft can often spot the flash long before you'd be able to see the aircraft itself.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, Signaling Devices section (manual page ~131); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: When should a red rocket parachute flare be used, and what are its key performance figures?

choices.text: When a vessel is spotted far away on the horizon; it reaches roughly 1,000 feet and burns bright red for no less than 40 seconds
When an aircraft is spotted overhead; it burns for at least 1 minute and marks your exact position
choices.misconception: Describes the hand flare's use case and figures, not the red rocket parachute flare's.

choices.text: Only at night, reaching about 100 feet and burning for 10 seconds
choices.misconception: Understates the altitude by an order of magnitude and the burn time considerably; also wrongly restricts use to nighttime only.

choices.text: Its use case and performance figures are identical to a buoyant smoke signal's
choices.misconception: Conflates two very different signaling devices with different use cases (a distant vessel vs. daylight-only visibility) and different performance figures.

hints: This flare gets high in the air and drifts as it descends -- good for a vessel that's still far away, rather than for marking your exact position.
questions.explanation: A red rocket parachute flare should be used when a vessel is spotted far away on the horizon, since it gets the signal up high (about 1,000 feet) where it can be seen from a greater distance. It burns bright red for no less than 40 seconds, with a parachute designed not to be damaged while it burns.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, Signaling Devices section (manual page ~131); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: Which best distinguishes a hand flare from a red rocket parachute flare?

choices.text: A hand flare is better for a spotted aircraft and marks your exact position; a parachute flare is better for a distant vessel and drifts as it descends
They are functionally identical, differing only in how they're held
choices.misconception: Contradicts the source's explicit distinctions between the two flares' best use cases and behavior after descent/immersion.

choices.text: A hand flare reaches higher altitude than a parachute flare
choices.misconception: Reverses which flare reaches high altitude -- the parachute flare is specifically designed to reach roughly 1,000 feet, while the hand flare stays at the survivor's position.

choices.text: Only the parachute flare continues burning after being immersed in water
choices.misconception: Reverses this fact -- it's the hand flare that's specifically noted as continuing to burn for 10 seconds after immersion in water.

hints: One flare stays right where you are; the other goes up high and drifts away from your position as it burns.
questions.explanation: A hand flare is better suited to a spotted aircraft (more likely to be noticed than a parachute flare) and marks your exact position, since it stays where you are. A red rocket parachute flare is better for a vessel still far away on the horizon, since it reaches high altitude for visibility -- but it drifts further from your actual position as it descends, unlike the hand flare.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, Signaling Devices section (manual page ~131); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: When can a buoyant smoke signal be used, and how long must it emit smoke?

choices.text: Daylight only; it must emit highly visible smoke for at least 3 minutes in calm water, continuing 10 seconds after being immersed in water
Day or night equally well; it must emit smoke for at least 3 minutes
choices.misconception: Ignores the explicit daylight-only limitation -- smoke cannot be seen once it gets dark.

choices.text: Daylight only; it must emit smoke for at least 40 seconds
choices.misconception: Confuses the smoke signal's duration with the red rocket parachute flare's separate 40-second minimum burn time.

choices.text: Night only, since smoke is most visible against a dark sky
choices.misconception: Reverses the actual restriction -- a buoyant smoke signal is explicitly a daylight-only device, since smoke cannot be seen once it gets dark.

hints: Smoke has an obvious visibility limitation once the light is gone.
questions.explanation: A buoyant smoke signal should only be used in daylight, since smoke cannot be seen once it gets dark. It emits highly visible smoke for at least 3 minutes while floating in calm water, and continues emitting smoke for 10 seconds after being immersed in water.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, Signaling Devices section (manual page ~131); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: How far can a whistle be heard in favorable weather conditions, compared to the human voice in calm weather?

choices.text: A whistle can be heard up to 4 miles away, versus only a few hundred yards for the voice
A whistle and the voice carry about the same distance
choices.misconception: Understates the whistle's advantage -- it carries dramatically farther than the human voice, which is limited to a few hundred yards even in calm weather.

choices.text: A whistle can be heard up to 4 miles away, but only works in clear daylight
choices.misconception: Understates the whistle's versatility -- it works in fog, at night, or during the day, not only in clear daylight.

choices.text: The voice actually carries farther than a whistle in most conditions
choices.misconception: Reverses the actual comparison -- a whistle carries much farther than the voice.

hints: Screaming will also make you hoarse and eventually lose your voice -- a whistle doesn't have that downside.
questions.explanation: A whistle can be heard up to 4 miles away in favorable weather conditions, versus only a few hundred yards for the human voice in calm weather. Unlike shouting, a whistle can be used over and over again without losing your voice, and works in fog, at night, or during the day.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, Signaling Devices section (manual page ~132); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: Why is rigging a radar reflector important for a rubber liferaft, and how should it be mounted?

choices.text: Rubber liferafts don't reflect radar waves efficiently on their own, so a reflector helps a ship's radar detect the raft; it should be mounted as high as possible
Rubber liferafts reflect radar very well already, and a reflector is mainly a backup for cloudy weather
choices.misconception: Reverses the actual problem -- rubber liferafts specifically do NOT reflect radar waves efficiently, which is exactly why a reflector is needed.

choices.text: It should be mounted as low as possible, close to the waterline, to avoid wind resistance
choices.misconception: Reverses the mounting guidance -- height improves detection, so the reflector should be mounted as high as possible, not low.

choices.text: A radar reflector replaces the need for any other signaling device once rigged
choices.misconception: Overstates the radar reflector's role -- the source describes it as a way to help ships detect the raft, not as a replacement for other signaling devices like flares or a mirror.

hints: Think about what a rubber raft's material does (or doesn't do) to radar waves, and how mounting height relates to detection at long range, the same way it does for a SART.
questions.explanation: Rubber liferafts do not reflect radar waves efficiently on their own, so rigging a radar reflector helps other ships notice the raft on radar -- even if a flare is missed or misread, radar detection can prevent a collision or help a ship find you. The reflector should be mounted as high as possible (e.g., atop the raft or lashed to an oar), since height improves detection range.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, Signaling Devices section (manual page ~132); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md

questions.prompt: What color dye marker is recognized as a valid distress signal?

choices.text: Any color
Only orange
choices.misconception: Confuses the dye marker signal (any color) with the specifically-orange smoke signal, a different item on the recognized distress-signal list.

choices.text: Only fluorescent green
choices.misconception: Invents a color restriction not described in the source, which specifies any color is acceptable for a dye marker.

choices.text: Dye markers are not a recognized distress signal
choices.misconception: Contradicts the source, which explicitly lists setting a dye marker of any color as a recognized distress signal.

hints: Unlike the smoke signal (which has a specific required color), this one has no color restriction at all.
questions.explanation: Setting a dye marker of any color is one of the 16 recognized international distress signals -- unlike the smoke signal, which specifically must be orange, the dye marker has no color restriction.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 5 manual source intake, International Distress Signals section (manual page ~130); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-14-chapter-5-source-intake.md
